stories.title: Houthis' Red Sea 'embargo' turns real — Saudi oil tankers start U-turning as the threat to enter the US-Iran war grows
stories.what_happened: By Tuesday, July 21, the threat stopped being just words: at least five tankers carrying Saudi crude reversed course in the southern Red Sea rather than risk the Bab al-Mandeb strait, the narrow chokepoint Houthi fighters would need to control to make good on the embargo. Two named examples — the Xin Long Yang, loaded with 2 million barrels for China, and the Rodos, carrying about 700,000 barrels for India — both abandoned the strait for the far longer Suez Canal route. Saudi Arabia rejected the Houthi embargo claim as 'disinformation,' saying it has supported Yemen with aid and fuel, and the UN called for immediate de-escalation.
key_points: At least 5 Saudi-linked oil tankers have now reversed course in the Red Sea
Two big examples: a China-bound ship carrying 2 million barrels, an India-bound one with 700,000
Ships are taking the long way round via the Suez Canal instead of the Bab al-Mandeb strait
Analysts warn oil could push above $100 a barrel if the rerouting continues

depth.background: Yemen's Houthis are an Iran-backed armed group that controls most of northern Yemen after a decade-long civil war against a Saudi-led coalition. Since the Gaza war began in 2023, they've repeatedly attacked Red Sea shipping they tie to Israel, forcing many shipping firms to avoid the route already. On Monday, July 20, Houthi spokesman Yahya Saree declared an immediate 'maritime embargo' on Saudi Arabia, saying it was retaliation for a Saudi blockade of Yemen and a strike on Sanaa's airport — an announcement made with no real detail on how it would be enforced.

thread.previously: Yesterday, Houthi spokesman Yahya Saree declared an immediate naval blockade of Saudi Arabia, framing it as retaliation for a Saudi blockade of Yemen and a strike on Sanaa's airport.
thread.changed: The blockade turned real: at least five Saudi-linked oil tankers have now actually reversed course away from the Bab al-Mandeb strait, and analysts warn oil could break above $100 a barrel if the rerouting continues.

prev_ref.title: Yemen's Houthis declare a naval blockade of Saudi Arabia — a new front opens in the widening Gulf war

stories.title: Hashmatullah Shahidi resigns as Afghanistan's ODI captain
stories.what_happened: ESPNcricinfo reported on 21 July that Shahidi has resigned as Afghanistan's ODI captain, ending a tenure in which he led the side in 55 ODIs. The report did not detail his reasoning, and the Afghanistan Cricket Board has not yet named who will replace him.
key_points: Steps down after leading Afghanistan in 55 One Day Internationals (ODIs)
His reign included famous 2023 World Cup wins over England, Pakistan and Sri Lanka
Comes weeks after Ibrahim Zadran replaced Rashid Khan as Afghanistan's T20I captain
Afghanistan Cricket Board has not yet named a successor

depth.background: Hashmatullah Shahidi, a steady middle-order batter, took over as Afghanistan's ODI and Test captain in 2021 after the board removed Asghar Afghan. Under him, Afghanistan grew from a plucky underdog into a genuine 50-over threat, peaking at the 2023 World Cup in India where they beat three former champions — England, Pakistan and Sri Lanka — a run that put the cricket world on notice. That rise has come alongside broader turbulence in Afghan cricket leadership: after the team crashed out in the group stage of the 2026 T20 World Cup, the board already replaced Rashid Khan with Ibrahim Zadran as T20I captain.

stories.title: Punjab Police say they've foiled a UK-directed plot to bomb a train — 17 arrested over the Moga police-station blast
stories.what_happened: On July 21, 2026, Punjab's Director General of Police Gaurav Yadav announced that police had 'busted' the conspiracy behind the July 8 Moga blast, arresting 17 people and recovering an IED, an electronic detonator, explosive material, and a 9mm Glock pistol with a magazine. Police say the plot's alleged mastermind, Iqbal Singh (alias Ravi Dhaliwal), directed the group from the UK, and that the accused were also planning to plant an explosive device on a train running between Ferozepur and Jammu. An FIR was registered under the Bharatiya Nyaya Sanhita (India's new criminal code), the Explosive Substances Act, the anti-terror UAPA law, and property-damage statutes.
key_points: 17 people arrested; an IED, detonator and a 9mm pistol recovered
Alleged mastermind: Iqbal Singh, alias Ravi Dhaliwal, based in the UK
Group allegedly planned to plant an IED on a Ferozepur–Jammu train
Case traces back to a grenade attack on Moga's Sadar police station on July 8

depth.background: On July 8, 2026, an explosion — suspected to be a hand grenade — went off outside the Sadar police station in Moga, Punjab, causing no injuries. It fits a pattern of recent attacks on Punjab police stations and rail infrastructure that investigators have increasingly linked to Pakistan-backed, diaspora-directed militant networks, run by handlers abroad rather than fighters based locally.

stories.title: Supreme Court orders a fresh trial in the 1996 Rajasthan bus bombing — 29 years after 14 people were killed
stories.what_happened: On July 21, 2026, a three-judge Supreme Court bench (Justices Vikram Nath, Sanjay Karol and Sandeep Mehta) set aside both Hameed's conviction and the High Court's 2019 order upholding it, ruling that he had been denied effective legal representation during the original trial. The Court ordered a genuinely fresh trial — not merely a review of the existing evidence — at a new fast-track special court in Jaipur, to be completed within a year, with Hameed guaranteed a lead lawyer of at least 10 years' experience. It also upheld the acquittals of three co-accused, including Pappu alias Salim, who remains on parole.
key_points: SC set aside Abdul Hameed's death sentence and ordered a complete retrial
Reason: he was denied effective, competent legal representation at trial
Co-accused Pappu alias Salim stays acquitted; two other acquittals also upheld
A new fast-track special court in Jaipur must finish the retrial within a year

depth.background: On May 22, 1996, an RDX bomb tore through a Rajasthan Roadways bus near Samleti village on the Agra–Bikaner route, killing 14 people and injuring 37 — a day after a similar bombing in Delhi's Lajpat Nagar market killed 13. Investigators blamed a terror conspiracy tied to the Jammu & Kashmir Islamic Front and Harkat-ul-Ansar militant groups, allegedly timed to disrupt Jammu & Kashmir's 1996 state elections. Abdul Hameed was convicted only in 2014 — 18 years after the blast — and sentenced to death; Rajasthan's High Court upheld that conviction in 2019.

stories.title: Trump readies fresh tariffs on dozens of countries as his 10% global tariff truce expires this Friday
stories.what_happened: On July 21, the Financial Times reported — per Reuters, which said it could not independently verify the FT's account — that Trump is preparing to announce fresh tariffs on dozens of countries, potentially as soon as this week. The trigger is that his existing 10% global tariff is due to expire this Friday, July 24. The FT says the most likely new duties would start at around that same 10% rate, but the administration is also pursuing separate trade investigations that could give it legal grounds to impose higher tariffs on specific countries later.
key_points: FT reports Trump could unveil new tariffs on dozens of countries as soon as this week
His current across-the-board 10% global tariff expires Friday, July 24
New duties are expected to start around that same 10% level
Separate trade investigations could let the administration push some rates higher later

depth.background: Since returning to office, Trump has used a temporary, blanket tariff — a flat import tax applied to goods from almost every country — as a baseline trade tool, alongside country-specific deals and higher 'reciprocal' tariffs matched to what other nations charge US exporters (like the 50% tariff just placed on Canada over autos, alcohol and cheese). These blanket tariffs have been repeatedly set to expire and then renewed or replaced, becoming a recurring deadline that markets and trading partners now watch closely.

stories.title: Ben Stokes marks his return from Test retirement with a stunning comeback century for Durham
stories.what_happened: Playing for Durham against Derbyshire in the One-Day Cup at Derby on 21 July, Stokes made his first appearance in competitive cricket since retiring from Tests. Despite carrying a leg injury, he produced a matchwinning century that carried Durham to victory, drawing a crowd of around 1,300 to a domestic fixture that would normally attract far less attention.
key_points: First competitive innings since retiring from Test cricket
Battled a leg injury to score a matchwinning hundred
Powered Durham to victory over Derbyshire in the One-Day Cup
His innings overshadowed The Hundred's opening night the same evening

depth.background: Ben Stokes, England's Test captain and one of the sport's biggest names since his match-winning heroics in the 2019 World Cup final, recently stepped away from Test cricket. Durham is his boyhood county, and rather than featuring in this year's Hundred (English cricket's flagship T20 franchise league), he chose to return to the game through the more traditional 50-over County format — a lower-key stage for such a high-profile comeback.

stories.title: A judge approves Anthropic's $1.5 billion payout to authors — the biggest copyright settlement ever, over AI trained on pirated books
stories.what_happened: On Monday, US District Judge Araceli Martinez-Olguin gave final approval to a $1.5 billion class-action settlement, calling it 'meaningful relief' for writers. Authors will receive roughly $3,000 for each affected book, across more than 482,000 works — about 91% of which have already been claimed. Anthropic must also destroy the pirated copies it used. Plaintiff lawyers called it 'the largest known copyright recovery in history.'
key_points: Largest known copyright recovery in history
About $3,000 per book to 500,000+ authors
Anthropic must destroy the pirated book copies
Landmark test of AI vs copyright law

depth.background: Anthropic is the San Francisco AI company behind the Claude chatbot. To build such a system, AI firms feed it enormous amounts of text so it learns to write and reason. A group of authors sued Anthropic in 2024, saying it had trained Claude on their books — obtained from pirate websites that host stolen copies for free. Last year the judge drew a crucial line: training AI on books you legally own can be 'fair use' (legal), but downloading millions of pirated copies to do it is not. That ruling pushed Anthropic to settle rather than risk a far larger jury verdict.

stories.title: The Hundred is back: defending champions Sunrisers Leeds thrash MI London to open the 2026 season
stories.what_happened: The 2026 edition opened on Tuesday, 21 July at the Kennington Oval in London with the women's match. MI London posted 107 for 8, and Sunrisers Leeds chased it down comfortably, reaching 109 for 3 with 31 balls to spare to win by seven wickets. Australia's Phoebe Litchfield top-scored with a brisk 43 from 26 balls and was named player of the match; the champions' side also included India all-rounder Deepti Sharma. Sunrisers were, however, left sweating on the fitness of England seamer Kate Cross.
key_points: New season of England's 100-ball league begins at The Oval
Sunrisers Leeds women win by 7 wickets, chasing 108 with ease
Australia's Phoebe Litchfield stars with 43 off 26 balls
India's Deepti Sharma features in the champions' line-up

depth.background: The Hundred is a short, fast cricket tournament run by the England and Wales Cricket Board (ECB) since 2021. Each innings is exactly 100 balls (instead of the usual 120 in a T20 match), and eight city-based teams play parallel men's and women's competitions across mid-summer. It was designed to be simple, quick and family-friendly to pull in new fans — and it now runs alongside a major commercial shake-up, with the ECB having sold stakes in the eight franchises to private investors, several of them linked to owners of India's IPL teams. Sunrisers Leeds' women went in as the reigning 2025 champions.

stories.title: McLaren finally brings its big upgrade — and its unusual 'Macarena' rear wing — to this weekend's Hungarian GP
stories.what_happened: McLaren confirmed it will unleash a large upgrade package at this weekend's Hungarian Grand Prix (24–26 July) at the Hungaroring near Budapest, which it has long marked as its main chance to claw back performance before F1's summer break. As part of that push, it will finally trial an experimental rear wing — nicknamed the 'Macarena' because it changes shape as the car moves — during Friday's practice sessions. The team had planned to test the wing earlier, at the Austrian GP, but decided it needed more development first.
key_points: McLaren's 2026 car has been off the pace all year
A 'significant' upgrade package lands at the Hungarian GP
It will trial an upside-down, shape-shifting 'Macarena' rear wing in practice
Team admits its car was 2–3 months behind on aerodynamics

depth.background: McLaren won both Formula 1 world titles in 2024 and 2025, but the sport tore up its rulebook for 2026 — all-new cars with a 50/50 split between engine and electric power. McLaren's new car, the MCL40, started the season badly: the team has openly admitted its design was two to three months behind where it needed to be on aerodynamics, the science of shaping a car so the air flowing over it presses it onto the track for grip. That has left drivers Lando Norris and Oscar Piastri fighting in the midfield instead of for wins.

stories.title: F1's rookie rule kicks in at the Hungarian GP — Piastri, Bearman and Colapinto all sit out first practice
stories.what_happened: At this weekend's Hungarian Grand Prix, three regular race drivers are stepping aside for Friday's first practice session to satisfy this rule. Leonardo Fornaroli, the reigning Formula 2 champion and McLaren's protégé, again takes over from Oscar Piastri. Toyota-backed Ryo Hirakawa replaces Oliver Bearman at Haas, and Paul Aron — on his third outing this year — takes Franco Colapinto's Alpine seat.
key_points: McLaren's Oscar Piastri, Haas's Oliver Bearman and Alpine's Franco Colapinto all miss FP1 at the Hungaroring
Their seats go to juniors: Leonardo Fornaroli (McLaren), Ryo Hirakawa (Haas) and Paul Aron (Alpine)
F1 rules force every team to hand two FP1 sessions per car per year to a rookie
It's Fornaroli's second FP1 outing of 2026, Aron's third — both are proving themselves for a future seat

depth.background: F1 cars are so expensive and complex that teams almost never let an unproven driver near one in an actual race. So the sport's rulebook forces the issue: every team must give a 'rookie' — someone who has driven in two or fewer F1 races — a run in first practice (FP1) at least twice per car, every season. It's how young drivers from a team's junior academy get real seat time in a current F1 car instead of just simulators.

stories.title: F1's next generation duels it out: Tsolov stretches his F2 points lead as the junior title fight tightens at Spa
stories.what_happened: After the Spa-Francorchamps round of the 2026 F2 season, Red Bull's Nikola Tsolov leads the drivers' standings with 161 points, built on six wins this year including a run of three in a row — a record for the series, previously only matched in its GP2-era predecessor. Alpine Academy's Gabriele Mini sits second on 134, with Ferrari Driver Academy's Rafael Camara third (125) and Alpine's Alex Dunne fourth (108).
key_points: Red Bull junior Nikola Tsolov leads Formula 2 on 161 points, 27 clear of second place
He's won six races this season, including three in a row — a Formula 2 record
Alpine's Gabriele Mini (134), Ferrari junior Rafael Camara (125) and Alpine's Alex Dunne (108) chase him
Eight of 14 rounds are done — the standings were set after this weekend's Spa-Francorchamps round

depth.background: Formula 2 is the main feeder championship directly below F1 — nearly every current F1 driver raced here first. Most F2 drivers are funded by an F1 team's junior 'academy' program (Red Bull, Ferrari, Alpine and others all run one), which pays for their seat and grooms them as a future F1 prospect. A dominant F2 season is usually the strongest evidence a young driver can present when lobbying for a real F1 seat.

stories.title: England's Jacob Bethell ruled out of The Hundred with a knee injury — and his Pakistan Test place is now in doubt
stories.what_happened: After an MRI scan on Monday, the England and Wales Cricket Board confirmed on Tuesday, 21 July that Bethell has been ruled out of the entire 2026 Hundred with a right-knee injury, with no recovery timeline given yet. That ends his planned stint captaining Birmingham Phoenix — South Africa's Donovan Ferreira has been named to lead the side instead for their opening game on Friday. More significantly, it casts doubt over his availability for England's Test series in Pakistan, where the first Test at Headingley is scheduled to start on 19 August.
key_points: Rising all-rounder hurt his right knee during the India ODI series
MRI on Monday ruled him out of the whole Hundred season
He loses the chance to captain Birmingham Phoenix
Availability for August's Test series in Pakistan now uncertain

depth.background: Jacob Bethell is one of England's most talked-about young all-rounders — a left-hand batter and left-arm spinner tipped as a future leader, who had been handed the captaincy of Birmingham Phoenix for this year's Hundred. He picked up the knee problem during England's recently concluded one-day international (ODI) series against India, which England won 2-1.

stories.title: Microsoft strikes a multibillion-dollar deal to power up Mistral — Europe's bid to run frontier AI on its own soil
stories.what_happened: On Tuesday, July 21, Microsoft and Mistral announced an expanded partnership that Microsoft describes as a multibillion-dollar commitment to build AI computing capacity across Europe. Microsoft will fund Mistral's growing fleet of GPUs — the specialised chips that train and run AI — including thousands of Nvidia's latest 'Vera Rubin' processors installed in data centres in France. Crucially, European enterprises and regulated industries (banks, hospitals, governments) will be able to run Mistral's models on 'Azure Local' — including fully disconnected servers with no internet connection — so sensitive data never leaves their own premises. Microsoft is providing the money but is NOT taking a new equity stake, a deliberate choice given the earlier EU scrutiny.
key_points: Microsoft will fund Mistral's European data-centre build-out — a multibillion-dollar bet
Thousands of Nvidia's newest 'Vera Rubin' chips go into French data centres
European firms can now run these AI models offline, on their own servers
Microsoft funds the compute but takes NO new ownership stake — dodging EU antitrust

depth.background: Mistral AI is a Paris-based startup, founded in 2023, that has become Europe's flagship answer to America's OpenAI and Google — it builds large language models (the technology behind chatbots like ChatGPT). Europe has grown anxious that almost all cutting-edge AI, and the giant data centres that run it, are controlled by a handful of US firms — a dependence governments increasingly see as a strategic risk. Microsoft already owned a small slice of Mistral from a 2024 deal, an arrangement EU regulators had eyed for antitrust concerns (whether it let a US giant quietly capture Europe's champion).

stories.title: Ukraine erupts in wartime protests demanding army chief Syrsky be sacked — Zelensky is already interviewing replacements
stories.what_happened: For a fifth straight day, several thousand people gathered outside the presidential office in Kyiv, demanding that Fedorov be reinstated and that Syrsky be dismissed by Friday. Fedorov has publicly accused Syrsky of blocking the reforms Ukraine needs 'because the war has changed completely' — meaning cheap drones and software now decide battles more than traditional tactics. President Zelensky, feeling the pressure, has confirmed he is reviewing candidates to replace Syrsky and has reportedly begun interviewing people for the role.
key_points: Crowds have rallied for days outside Zelensky's office
Trigger: a popular, drone-focused defence minister was dropped
Protesters want army chief Syrsky gone by Friday
Rare public dissent inside a country at war

depth.background: Ukraine has been fighting Russia's full-scale invasion since February 2022, and for most of that time public criticism of its own leaders has been muted — the country closes ranks in wartime. Oleksandr Syrsky has been commander-in-chief (the top general running the whole war effort) since early 2024, when Zelensky replaced the hugely popular Valery Zaluzhny with him. The current anger was lit by a government reshuffle in which Mykhailo Fedorov — a 35-year-old former tech minister widely credited with Ukraine's game-changing drone programme — was pushed out of the defence job.

stories.title: Alonso says his F1 future rests on the rules, not on Aston Martin — and 2027's engine shake-up is the real deciding factor
stories.what_happened: Speaking at last weekend's Belgian Grand Prix, Alonso said his decision about racing next year will hinge on the rules and how enjoyable the cars are to drive — not on how well Aston Martin performs. He said driving the current hybrid cars at tracks like Spa or Silverstone 'is not what I'm dreaming of.' Aston Martin is pinning hopes on a big upgrade at this weekend's Hungarian GP to help persuade him to stay.
key_points: Alonso, 44, has a contract expiring at the end of 2026
Says his stay depends on whether the cars are fun to drive, not team results
Aston Martin is banking on a Hungary upgrade to keep him
2027 rules will tilt engines back toward petrol power

depth.background: Fernando Alonso is a two-time world champion and, at 44, one of the oldest drivers on the grid. He races for Aston Martin, a team that has struggled badly with F1's all-new 2026 cars — its package has been overweight and unreliable. His contract runs out at the end of this year, so whether he keeps racing into 2027 (past his 45th birthday) has become a major talking point.

stories.title: India's Navy commissions INS Malvan, a 'submarine hunter' built to track Chinese and Pakistani subs in the Indian Ocean
stories.what_happened: On Tuesday, July 21, the Indian Navy commissioned INS Malvan, an anti-submarine warfare shallow-water craft — a compact vessel specialised in detecting, tracking and attacking submarines close to the coast and in shallow seas where larger warships are less effective. The Navy framed the induction as part of raising its fleet to counter the Chinese and Pakistani underwater threat in the Indian Ocean Region (IOR).
key_points: New warship is designed to detect and hunt enemy submarines in shallow coastal waters
Aimed at China's growing and Pakistan's underwater presence in the Indian Ocean
Part of a homegrown fleet India is building up under 'Make in India'
Named after Malvan, a coastal town in Maharashtra

depth.background: The Indian Ocean is India's strategic backyard — most of its trade and almost all of its oil imports cross it — and for over a decade China has steadily expanded its naval footprint there, sending submarines and warships far from home, while Pakistan has been building up its own submarine fleet, much of it with Chinese help. Submarines are the hardest threat to counter because they hide underwater and can strike ships or blockade ports unseen. To answer that, India has been inducting a class of small, purpose-built anti-submarine vessels made in Indian shipyards, part of a broader push to build warships at home rather than import them.

stories.title: Novo Nordisk sues Eli Lilly, saying its weight-loss ads mislead patients about which drug works better
stories.what_happened: On Tuesday, July 21, 2026, Novo Nordisk sued Eli Lilly in US District Court in New Jersey, alleging false advertising and unfair competition under the federal Lanham Act and state laws. Novo says Lilly's nationwide ads pit the highest approved dose of Zepbound against a lower, older dose of Wegovy — one commercial claims patients lose 50 pounds on Lilly's drug versus 33 pounds on Novo's — while ignoring Novo's newer high-dose (7.2 mg) Wegovy, approved this year, which narrows the gap. Novo says it sent a cease-and-desist letter in April; Lilly instead added what Novo calls an inadequate disclaimer. Novo wants a court order pulling the ads and forcing a corrective campaign, and will seek an injunction if Lilly does not comply.
key_points: Novo says Lilly's ads compare its top dose to Novo's older, weaker one
The disputed ad claims 50 lbs lost on Zepbound vs 33 on Wegovy
Novo wants the ads pulled and a 'corrective' campaign run
It's the latest front in the two giants' obesity-drug war

depth.background: Two companies dominate the booming market for GLP-1 drugs — a class of medicines (first made for diabetes) that curb appetite and produce large weight loss. Novo Nordisk makes Ozempic and Wegovy (both semaglutide); Eli Lilly makes Mounjaro and Zepbound (both tirzepatide). Together they form a near-duopoly worth tens of billions of dollars, and Lilly's Zepbound has been winning market share partly by advertising bigger average weight loss than Novo's drugs.

stories.title: India's first private rocket reached orbit — now its maker wants to launch one every month
stories.what_happened: On July 18, 2026, Skyroot's Vikram-1 lifted off from the Satish Dhawan Space Centre at Sriharikota — after a 35-minute countdown hold for a last-minute technical check — and reached its planned low Earth orbit about 450 km up, releasing several small technology-demonstration satellites. That made Skyroot the first private Indian company to put a payload into orbit from Indian soil. In an interview aired on July 21, CEO Pawan Kumar Chandana said the next goal is to ramp up to roughly one launch a month.
key_points: Skyroot's Vikram-1 is India's first private rocket to reach orbit
It flew from Sriharikota on July 18, hitting a ~450 km orbit
CEO now says 'one launch a month' is the next goal
A milestone for India's newly opened private space sector

depth.background: For decades every Indian rocket was built and flown by ISRO, the government space agency. In 2020 India opened space to private companies and created a regulator, IN-SPACe, to license and encourage them. Hyderabad-based Skyroot Aerospace, founded in 2018, was the first to fly a private Indian rocket — the small suborbital Vikram-S in November 2022, which went up and came straight back down without circling the Earth. The far bigger prize was 'orbital' flight: actually placing a satellite into a stable path around the planet, which takes vastly more power and precision.

stories.title: Lebanon's army moves into the south as Israel starts pulling back — the first real test of a US-brokered deal
stories.what_happened: On Tuesday, July 21, 2026, Lebanese army troops began deploying in the southern town of Zawtar al-Gharbiyeh after Israeli forces pulled out — the first handover under the US plan, nicknamed the 'pilot zone' program. The US State Department said the Lebanese army was also taking over security in the nearby villages of Froun and Srifa. Lebanese President Joseph Aoun was due in Washington later the same day to meet President Donald Trump. But Hezbollah has rejected the deal and refuses to disarm, and there is still no timetable for Israel's full withdrawal.
key_points: Israeli troops left a first town; the Lebanese army moved in
Part of a US 'pilot zone' plan to unwind the occupation
The deal asks Lebanon's army to disarm Hezbollah in the south
President Aoun heads to Washington to meet Trump

depth.background: Israel and Hezbollah — the powerful Iran-backed armed group and political party in Lebanon — fought a bruising war that left Israeli troops holding a strip of southern Lebanon several kilometres deep. Washington has been brokering a plan to unwind that occupation in stages: Israel withdraws town by town, and in return Lebanon's national army moves in and strips Hezbollah of its weapons in those areas.

stories.title: France is on the verge of becoming the first country in Europe to ban children under 15 from social media
stories.what_happened: France's National Assembly (the lower house of parliament) and the Senate (the upper house) reached a compromise on the bill's exact wording on Monday, July 20, clearing the way for a final adoption vote expected on Tuesday, which lawmakers believe will pass despite objections from parties on the left. The bill would block anyone under 15 from Facebook, Snapchat, TikTok and YouTube and require platforms to run age-verification systems, with exceptions for educational sites and online encyclopaedias. Under-15s could not open new accounts from September 1, and platforms would get four more months to close accounts children already have. Because of an EU rule requiring member states to notify Brussels first, the law could not take effect before August 10 even if adopted this week. Digital Minister Anne Le Henanff said France would become 'the first country in Europe to introduce a digital age limit,' and President Emmanuel Macron has backed the push.
key_points: Upper and lower houses reached a compromise; a final vote is expected this week
The ban covers TikTok, Snapchat, Facebook and YouTube; platforms must check users' ages
Under-15s couldn't open new accounts from September 1
Left-wing critics and many teens oppose it, and enforcement is the hard part

depth.background: Governments worldwide are wrestling with growing evidence that heavy social media use can harm children's mental health and expose them to bullying and harmful content. Australia passed a world-first law in 2024 banning under-16s from social media, and France has been pushing the European Union to set a 'digital age of majority' of 15. This debate runs in parallel with the trial in the US where the state of Tennessee accuses Meta of designing Instagram to hook children.

stories.title: Flash floods tear through a remote Afghan valley — at least 23 dead, 100+ missing as a mountain town is buried in mud
stories.what_happened: Flash floods struck the provincial capital, Parun, and surrounding areas of Nuristan on Monday, July 20. Afghanistan's National Disaster Management Authority reported at least 23 people killed, around 80 injured, and more than 100 still missing as of Tuesday. Powerful torrents of water carried large boulders and thick mud through the town, destroying hotels and dozens of shops and severely damaging roads. Rescue crews were digging through rubble on Tuesday, and officials warned the death toll is likely to rise.
key_points: Sudden floods hit Nuristan's capital, Parun, in Afghanistan's isolated east
At least 23 dead, about 80 injured, and more than 100 still missing
Torrents dumped huge boulders and thick mud, flattening hotels and shops
Rescue is slow — the province is cut off by mountains and wrecked roads

depth.background: Nuristan is one of Afghanistan's most remote and mountainous provinces, tucked against the Pakistan border in the country's northeast, with steep valleys and few paved roads. Afghanistan is highly exposed to sudden 'flash' floods — walls of water that surge down dry or narrow valleys after intense rain — because decades of war have left it with little flood defence, weak early-warning systems, and deforested slopes that can't soak up rain. The Taliban-run government has limited resources and is largely cut off from international aid, so disaster response is thin.

stories.title: A day after Delhi's crackdown, India's youth-led 'Cockroach' protest regroups — Congress demands Modi and his education minister resign
stories.what_happened: On Tuesday, July 21, the protesters regrouped at Jantar Mantar in central Delhi and vowed not to leave, entering day two of the sit-in. Officials said Monday's clashes left roughly 60 protesters and 118 police injured; by Tuesday morning 79 injured students had been discharged, but a 20-year-old woman remained in intensive care on a ventilator at Ram Manohar Lohia Hospital. The Delhi High Court, citing his deteriorating health, allowed Wangchuk to be shifted from the government-run Safdarjung Hospital to the private Medanta hospital, with the Centre raising 'no objection.' Rahul and Priyanka Gandhi led a Congress protest to Prime Minister Narendra Modi's residence demanding that Modi and Education Minister Dharmendra Pradhan resign; several Congress leaders were detained, and a Union minister came out to speak with Rahul. Modi, for his part, said preventing paper leaks was 'a national responsibility' and that the government had taken 'strict action' against those behind the NEET leak.
key_points: Gen-Z 'Cockroach' protesters refuse to leave Jantar Mantar for a second day
A 20-year-old woman injured in Monday's police action is in ICU on a ventilator
Delhi High Court lets hunger-striking activist Sonam Wangchuk move to a private hospital
Rahul and Priyanka Gandhi lead a Congress march to PM Modi's residence, demanding resignations

depth.background: The 'Cockroach' movement — its supporters wear the name as a badge, saying the powerful treat ordinary young Indians as pests — is a mostly leaderless, Gen-Z protest that erupted over corruption, a shortage of jobs, and above all the integrity of India's exams. The immediate trigger is the alleged leak of NEET, the single national medical-entrance test that decides which of the millions who sit it get a medical-college seat; a leak means cheats can buy a future that honest students earn. The veteran Ladakhi activist and education reformer Sonam Wangchuk has been on an indefinite hunger strike since June 28 in solidarity, and on Monday, July 20, a march the protesters called 'Sansad Chalo' ('March to Parliament') was broken up with tear gas and baton (lathi) charges as thousands tried to reach Parliament.

thread.previously: On Monday, thousands of 'Cockroach' protesters marching from Jantar Mantar toward Parliament were stopped by police with tear gas and baton charges, and dozens on both sides were injured.
thread.changed: A day later the protest didn't collapse — it regrouped for a second day; a woman hurt on Monday is now in the ICU, the courts ordered hunger-striker Sonam Wangchuk moved to a private hospital, and Rahul and Priyanka Gandhi escalated it into a formal Congress demand for Modi and his education minister to resign.

prev_ref.title: 'Cockroach' march to Parliament turns violent — police baton-charge protesters in Delhi

stories.title: Mercedes will use team orders to protect a race win — but won't make Russell surrender to title-leader Antonelli
stories.what_happened: Team principal Toto Wolff spelled out where Mercedes draws the line. The team WILL impose orders if Antonelli and Russell are fighting each other so hard that they risk handing a win to a rival — 'we are not going to lose time with each other,' he said. But it will NOT demote Russell to a support role just because Antonelli is winning the title: 'We would never take a victory away from any of the drivers. So, if it is George who is ahead, even though he is behind in the championship, at that stage of the season, we wouldn't do that.' The trigger was the Spanish GP in Barcelona, where the two spent six laps scrapping for the lead and lost about 13 seconds to Lewis Hamilton, letting Ferrari threaten the win.
key_points: Wolff: team orders only to stop the pair losing a win to rivals
He refuses to make Russell a number-two, despite a 50-point gap
Rooted in Barcelona, where their 6-lap duel cost 13 seconds
Both keep racing freely for now — even the title leader

depth.background: Mercedes has the fastest car in Formula 1 this season, but its two drivers are pulling in different directions. Rookie sensation Kimi Antonelli leads the drivers' championship and, after winning Sunday's Belgian Grand Prix at Spa, sits 50 points clear of team-mate George Russell, whose year has been wrecked by reliability problems. That imbalance raises the sport's oldest dilemma: when two drivers share a team, does the team let them race, or order one to move aside? 'Team orders' are exactly that — an instruction from the pit wall telling one driver to hold position or let the other by, usually to protect points.

stories.title: Punjab farmers march on Delhi against the India–US trade deal as Haryana seals the Shambhu border
stories.what_happened: On Tuesday, July 21, farmers from Punjab set off for a mahapanchayat (a large public assembly) at Kisan Ghat in Delhi, organised by the Desh Bachao Morcha, which says nearly 550 farmer unions and groups are joining. Haryana sealed the Shambhu border with barricades and cemented blocks over the Ghaggar river bridge, and some farmer leaders were detained — moves unions call an attempt to stop them reaching the capital. Leader Sarwan Singh Pandher said farmers want the India–US deal completely scrapped.
key_points: Farmers head to a mahapanchayat at Delhi's Kisan Ghat
Demand: scrap the proposed India–US trade agreement entirely
Haryana seals the Shambhu border; some leaders detained
Fear: cheap, subsidised US farm and dairy imports

depth.background: India and the US have been negotiating a trade agreement that could lower barriers to each other's goods. Indian farmers — a politically powerful group, especially in Punjab — have long resisted opening agriculture, remembering the 2020–21 protests that forced the government to repeal three farm laws. The Shambhu crossing on the Punjab–Haryana border has become the symbolic frontline where farmer marches to Delhi are repeatedly halted.

stories.title: A methane blast collapses a hydro-project tunnel in Sikkim — at least 7 killed, many feared trapped
stories.what_happened: Around midday on Monday, methane that had accumulated roughly 1.5 km inside the tunnel at Samardung (Jholungey area) in Namchi district exploded — reportedly during an attempt to release the trapped gas — setting off a landslide inside the tunnel that blocked the exit and trapped workers. At least seven bodies have been recovered (some outlets report eight), and several workers remain unaccounted for; the district collector said as many as 27 people may have been inside. Rescue teams are digging through the debris. Prime Minister Modi and Home Minister Amit Shah spoke with Chief Minister Prem Singh Tamang.
key_points: Gas explosion triggered a collapse inside an under-construction tunnel
At least 7 dead (some reports say 8); several workers still trapped
Site: NHPC's Teesta Stage-VI project, Namchi district
PM and Home Minister spoke to Sikkim's CM; rescue under way

depth.background: Sikkim, a small Himalayan state, is dotted with hydropower projects that channel the Teesta river through long tunnels bored into unstable mountain rock. NHPC — India's state-owned hydropower company (a PSU, or public sector undertaking) — is building the Teesta Stage-VI plant. Tunnelling here is hazardous: the geology is fragile, and pockets of natural methane gas can build up underground.

stories.title: Britain's new PM Andy Burnham names his cabinet and scraps VAT on electricity bills as his first act
stories.what_happened: On Tuesday, July 21, Burnham unveiled his cabinet (the top team of ministers who run government departments) and made his first big policy announcement. He is cutting VAT — value-added tax, a sales tax added to most purchases — on household electricity from 5% to 0% starting October 1, which the government says will take roughly £45 off the average annual bill and cost the Treasury about £850 million this year. To fund it, his team is scrapping the previous plan for a national Digital ID scheme. In the reshuffle, John Healey becomes chancellor (finance minister) in place of Rachel Reeves, Ed Miliband becomes foreign secretary, Wes Streeting takes defence and Shabana Mahmood stays as home secretary, while several Starmer-era figures — including former deputy PM David Lammy — leave government. Burnham chose not to appoint a deputy prime minister.
key_points: One day into the job, Burnham forms a government and sets his first policy
VAT on household electricity cut from 5% to 0% from October 1 — about £45 off the average bill
John Healey is the new chancellor; Reeves, Lammy and other Starmer-era figures are dropped
Paid for by scrapping the previous government's Digital ID scheme

depth.background: The UK has churned through leaders — Andy Burnham is its seventh prime minister in roughly a decade. He took over on Monday, July 20 after Keir Starmer resigned and Burnham won the leadership of the governing Labour Party, promising to 'rewire Britain' and ease a long cost-of-living squeeze. In Britain the prime minister isn't directly elected; the leader of the party that commands a majority in the House of Commons becomes PM, so a new leader can take office without a general election.

thread.previously: Yesterday we told readers Andy Burnham had won the Labour leadership and was being installed as prime minister on July 20, succeeding Keir Starmer and pledging to 'rewire Britain' — but he had not yet formed a government or set out any policy.
thread.changed: Today he actually built his government — naming John Healey chancellor and dropping Starmer-era figures like Reeves and Lammy — and made his first policy move: scrapping VAT on household electricity bills from October 1.

prev_ref.title: Andy Burnham becomes UK prime minister, promising to 'rewire Britain' — the fifth PM in four years

stories.title: SBI Mutual Fund's parent lists on the market after a $1 billion IPO — India's biggest fund house goes public
stories.what_happened: On Tuesday, July 21, SBI Funds Management made its stock-market debut on the NSE and BSE (India's two main exchanges) at about ₹613, a premium of roughly 7% over the ₹574 price set in its initial public offering (IPO — the first time a company sells shares to the public); some reports pegged the pop nearer 8-9%. The roughly $1 billion IPO was an offer for sale — existing owners SBI and Amundi sold part of their stakes rather than the company raising new money — and it drew bids worth about ₹2.97 lakh crore, oversubscribed around 42 times. The listing values the firm near $13 billion, making it India's second-most-valuable listed asset manager behind ICICI Prudential AMC; it manages about ₹29.5 lakh crore (~$395 billion) of assets.
key_points: SBI Funds Management shares debuted around 7-9% above the ₹574 issue price
The $1 billion IPO was oversubscribed about 42 times — bids worth ~₹2.97 lakh crore
Values the company near $13bn — India's No. 2 listed asset manager
It runs ₹29.5 lakh crore (~$395bn) of investors' money

depth.background: SBI Funds Management runs SBI Mutual Fund, India's largest mutual-fund house — a mutual fund pools money from many small investors and invests it in stocks and bonds on their behalf, for a fee. The company is a joint venture between State Bank of India, the country's biggest public-sector bank, and Amundi, a large European asset manager. Its listing rides a multi-year boom in ordinary Indians investing through SIPs (systematic investment plans — fixed monthly amounts auto-invested into funds), which has swelled the whole industry.

stories.title: A Filipino sailor is hurt as China's coast guard clashes with Manila's boats in the South China Sea
stories.what_happened: On July 20, near the grounded Sierra Madre, a Chinese inflatable carrying about eight personnel approached the outpost; when two Philippine navy boats moved to push it away, China's side 'reacted violently,' the Philippine military said — a coast guard member struck a Filipino sailor with a wooden baton, causing a head injury, and a navy rubber boat was damaged. Beijing disputes this, saying Philippine dinghies rammed its vessel and attacked its officers with oars and sticks. Washington condemned China's actions and reaffirmed the 2016 ruling.
key_points: Filipino navy sailor struck with a baton, suffering a head injury
Clash near Second Thomas Shoal, a long-running flashpoint
China and the Philippines blame each other for the ramming
US condemns Beijing's 'dangerous and aggressive' conduct

depth.background: The South China Sea is one of the world's busiest and most contested waterways, and China claims almost all of it — a claim a 2016 international tribunal rejected. A key flashpoint is Second Thomas Shoal (which the Philippines calls Ayungin), where Manila deliberately grounded an old warship, the BRP Sierra Madre, in 1999 to stake its claim and keeps a small garrison aboard. China's coast guard routinely tries to block Philippine resupply missions there.

stories.title: Australia recall Cummins, Hazlewood and Lyon from injury for the Bangladesh Test series
stories.what_happened: On July 21, Cricket Australia named a 13-member squad for the Test series in Bangladesh, with Cummins, Hazlewood and Lyon all returning after recovering from injury. All-rounder Cameron Green and Beau Webster were also included. Selectors framed the tour as the start of a demanding 12-month Test schedule, with the veterans' fitness a key talking point given the workload ahead.
key_points: Captain Pat Cummins returns to a 13-man squad
Fast bowler Josh Hazlewood and spinner Nathan Lyon also back
Cameron Green and Beau Webster included
Australia sit top of the World Test Championship table

depth.background: Australia are the reigning force in Test cricket and currently lead the World Test Championship (WTC) — the ICC's two-year league that crowns the best Test side. Their attack leans on three veterans: captain and spearhead Pat Cummins, fellow quick Josh Hazlewood, and off-spinner Nathan Lyon, all of whom had been managing injuries. A tour of Bangladesh opens a heavy year of Test cricket.

stories.title: Nicaragua's Ortega says the country will hold no more elections — 'never again'
stories.what_happened: Speaking to supporters at an event marking the 1979 revolution, Ortega declared there would be 'no more elections here' so that opponents could not 'seize power,' which he claimed the opposition — controlled, he says, by the United States — was trying to do. The remarks, reported widely on July 20-21, formalize what critics say was already true in practice: that no genuine vote is allowed. There was no immediate detail on any legal step to abolish elections, but the statement signals none will be held when his current term ends next year.
key_points: President Daniel Ortega, 80, says Nicaragua will not hold elections again
He rules jointly with his wife, Rosario Murillo, named 'co-president' last year
Frames it as blocking a US-backed opposition; critics call it the end of democracy
Removes any chance of a vote to challenge his rule when his term ends next year

depth.background: Daniel Ortega, a former Sandinista guerrilla, has led Nicaragua since 2007, winning a series of elections that international observers said were neither free nor fair. Over the past few years he has jailed or exiled opponents, shut down civil-society groups, and last year pushed through constitutional changes that made his wife, Rosario Murillo, 'co-president' and stretched the presidential term to six years. The Sandinista revolution he references overthrew the Somoza dictatorship in 1979 — the same kind of one-family rule critics now accuse Ortega of rebuilding.

stories.title: Trump hits Canada with 50% tariffs over autos, alcohol and cheese — and Carney vows to fight back
stories.what_happened: On Monday, July 20 (US time), Trump announced 50% tariffs on a broad range of Canadian goods — reportedly everything from cars to cement and hockey sticks — invoking Section 338 of the 1930 Tariff Act, a rarely used law that lets a president penalise countries deemed to discriminate against US commerce. He said Canada unfairly discriminates against American autos, alcohol and dairy; officials pointed to nearly all Canadian provinces halting sales of US liquor. The duties do not start for 30 days. Carney said Canada would protect its workers and counter the move while pushing to widen trade with other nations.
key_points: 50% tariff on a wide list of Canadian goods, from cars to hockey sticks
Takes effect in 30 days — a window for a deal
Trigger: provinces pulled US liquor and Canada's auto/dairy rules
Carney vows to counter and 'intensify' trade talks

depth.background: The US and Canada are each other's biggest trading partners and, on paper, are bound by USMCA — the free-trade pact (successor to NAFTA) that is supposed to let most goods cross the border tariff-free. But since returning to office, Trump has repeatedly used tariffs — taxes a country charges on imports — as a pressure tool, and relations with Canada's Prime Minister Mark Carney have been tense even as the two sat together at Sunday's World Cup final.

stories.title: Andrew and Tristan Tate are arrested in Miami and appear in a US court as Britain moves to extradite them on rape and trafficking charges
stories.what_happened: On Saturday, July 18, US Marshals arrested the brothers in Miami on a sealed warrant tied to a British extradition request. On Sunday the Crown Prosecution Service — the body that runs criminal prosecutions in England and Wales — revealed it had authorised a fresh set of charges after receiving new evidence, including further counts of rape, arranging or facilitating trafficking for sexual exploitation, assault, and offences relating to indecent images. That brings the total number of alleged victims in the UK case to seven. On Monday, July 20, the brothers appeared in a Miami federal court for the first hearing in the extradition case. They deny every allegation and say they will fight being sent to the UK.
key_points: US Marshals arrested the brothers in Miami on Saturday on a sealed UK warrant
UK prosecutors added dozens of new charges, including rape and sex trafficking
The case now involves seven alleged victims; both brothers deny everything
A Miami federal court opened extradition proceedings on Monday

depth.background: Andrew Tate (39) and his brother Tristan are dual US-UK citizens and among the most-followed — and most controversial — influencers online, known for Andrew's aggressive 'alpha male' videos aimed at young men. They have faced a criminal case in Romania since 2022 (accused of human trafficking, rape and running an organised crime group, all of which they deny). A Romanian travel ban on them was eased in early 2025 and they flew to Florida. Britain has been building a separate case against them through Bedfordshire Police for several years.

stories.title: Red Bull reportedly rejects an Abu Dhabi-backed $3bn bid for its second F1 team, Racing Bulls
stories.what_happened: According to reports circulating around the Belgian Grand Prix weekend, Red Bull rebuffed an offer worth as much as $3 billion for Racing Bulls. The bid was said to be led by US-based investor Dean Attew — a former adviser to ex-F1 boss Bernie Ecclestone — and financier Andrew Herriot, with money from several Abu Dhabi-based funds. It reportedly follows earlier approaches valuing the team at around $2–2.3 billion. Crucially, neither Red Bull nor the would-be buyers have publicly confirmed the talks, so the figures remain unverified.
key_points: Bid worth up to $3 billion for Racing Bulls, per reports
Led by ex-Ecclestone adviser Dean Attew, backed by Abu Dhabi funds
Red Bull is said to have turned it down
Neither side has publicly confirmed the approach

depth.background: Red Bull is unusual in Formula 1 for owning two teams: the flagship Red Bull Racing and a junior squad, currently branded Racing Bulls (based in Faenza, Italy), which bloods young talent before promoting them. F1 team values have soared as the sport's global popularity has boomed, drawing sovereign-wealth and private-equity money looking to buy in — and Red Bull has fielded approaches before.

stories.title: India signals the Indus Waters Treaty 'won't survive in its present form' — hardening its freeze on the water pact with Pakistan
stories.what_happened: A senior Indian official said the treaty 'will not remain operational in its present form' — the clearest signal yet that Delhi wants to renegotiate or replace it rather than simply return to the old arrangement. India argues Pakistan has not stopped sponsoring cross-border terrorism, so the pause stays. Government sources separately dismissed Pakistan's appeals to international forums as a 'victim card' that 'cannot change India's decision.' Pakistan insists the treaty remains binding and has said any move to cut its water share is a national-security red line.
key_points: Indian official: the 60-year-old treaty won't remain operational as-is
India put the pact 'in abeyance' after the 2025 Pahalgam terror attack
Delhi ties any revival to Pakistan ending cross-border terrorism
Pakistan calls its water share a red line and warns it could mean war

depth.background: The Indus Waters Treaty, signed in 1960 and brokered by the World Bank, splits the six rivers of the Indus basin between India and Pakistan and has survived every war between them for six decades — it is often called the world's most successful water-sharing deal. The waters of the three western rivers flow on to downstream Pakistan, where they irrigate the country's farm heartland. After a terror attack in Pahalgam, Kashmir, in April 2025, India put the treaty 'in abeyance' (paused, but not formally cancelled) and has kept it suspended since.

stories.title: The US and Iran are now trading direct strikes — three American troops killed, and Washington bombs Iran to reopen the Strait of Hormuz
stories.what_happened: The Pentagon says three US service members have been killed since Friday — two of them in an Iranian missile-and-drone attack on a base in Jordan. In response, President Trump ordered a new wave of airstrikes starting late Saturday, hitting Iranian military command centres, air-defence and coastal-surveillance sites, and missile and drone launch positions, saying the goal was to 'degrade Iran's ability to threaten commercial shipping' in the Strait of Hormuz. Iran struck back with attacks in Syria and Jordan, two oil tankers reportedly exploded in the strait, and Trump warned Tehran would 'pay many times over.' Iran says it will keep fighting until it has restored deterrence.
key_points: 3 US service members killed since Friday in Iran-linked attacks
US launches fresh airstrikes on Iranian military and coastal-radar sites
Two oil tankers reportedly exploded in the Strait of Hormuz
Trump vows Iran will 'pay many times over'; Tehran vows to fight on

depth.background: For weeks the US and Iran have been locked in an escalating standoff centred on the Strait of Hormuz — the narrow sea gate at the mouth of the Persian Gulf that carries roughly a fifth of the world's oil. Peace talks aimed at a 'memorandum of understanding' over the strait collapsed, and the two sides slid into near-daily tit-for-tat strikes. This weekend the confrontation crossed a dangerous line: US troops were killed in action, turning a mostly indirect conflict into direct combat between the two militaries.

stories.title: IndiGo orders over 1,000 jet engines from CFM — a giant bet on India's booming air travel
stories.what_happened: On Monday, July 20, IndiGo said it will place an order for more than 1,000 CFM LEAP aircraft engines — among the largest engine orders ever placed. The engines will power the hundreds of jets IndiGo has on order over the coming years. IndiGo's CEO-designate Willie Walsh said the LEAP engine's 'proven reliability' made it the ideal choice to support the airline's 'scale, operational resilience and sustainability ambitions'.
key_points: IndiGo to order 1,000+ CFM LEAP engines for its jets
Among the largest engine deals in aviation history
Backs India's fast-growing demand for air travel
CEO-designate Willie Walsh cites reliability and scale

depth.background: IndiGo is India's largest airline, flying more domestic passengers than any rival, and it holds one of the world's biggest order books for new planes. Aircraft need engines, and CFM International — a joint venture between America's GE Aerospace and France's Safran — makes the LEAP engine that powers the narrow-body jets (single-aisle planes used on short and medium routes) IndiGo flies. As India's middle class grows and more people fly for the first time, IndiGo is racing to add planes.

stories.title: A US judge freezes the $81 billion Paramount–Warner Bros merger — 12 states win a two-week pause on antitrust grounds
stories.what_happened: On Monday, July 20, a federal judge granted the states' request and ordered Paramount and Warner Bros. Discovery to halt their merger for at least two weeks while the court examines the antitrust challenge. Antitrust law is competition law — rules meant to stop a single company from getting so big it can crush rivals or overcharge customers. The order does not kill the deal; it presses pause so a judge can weigh whether the combined company would harm competition.
key_points: Federal judge halts the $81bn Paramount–Warner Bros deal
12 US states asked the court to block it on competition grounds
The freeze lasts at least two weeks while the case is heard
One of the biggest media mergers ever is now in limbo

depth.background: Paramount (home to CBS, MTV and the Paramount film studio) and Warner Bros. Discovery (home to HBO, CNN and the Warner film studio) agreed to combine into a single entertainment giant in a roughly $81 billion deal. Hollywood has been consolidating for years as streaming services like Netflix squeeze the old TV-and-movie business, pushing studios to merge for scale. A group of 12 US state governments objected, arguing the tie-up would hand one company too much control over what Americans watch and how much they pay.

stories.title: India signs on to the WTO's fisheries deal — a global pact to stop subsidies that empty the oceans
stories.what_happened: On Monday, July 20, 2026, India formally 'accepted' the WTO Agreement on Fisheries Subsidies — Commerce Secretary Rajesh Agrawal handed India's instrument of acceptance to the WTO's Director-General in Geneva, making India the 123rd member to join. By signing, India is now bound by rules that ban government subsidies for illegal, unreported and unregulated (IUU) fishing and for fishing stocks that are already overfished. India had spent years negotiating hard for carve-outs to protect its own small-scale fishers before agreeing to come on board.
key_points: India becomes the 123rd country to accept the WTO fisheries pact
The deal bans state money that funds illegal and overfishing
India fought for years to shield its small, poor fishers
Aim: a cleaner 'responsible exporter' image for its seafood trade

depth.background: The World Trade Organization (WTO) is the global body that writes and enforces the rules of international trade — 160-plus countries agreeing on what governments can and can't do to help their exporters. For over two decades its members argued over 'fisheries subsidies' — the money governments hand their fishing industries (cheap fuel, boat-building grants, and the like). Rich nations' subsidies let giant trawlers scour the seas and catch fish faster than they can breed, wiping out stocks that poorer coastal communities depend on. In June 2022, WTO members finally agreed a first-ever deal to curb the worst of this, and it became legally binding in September 2025 once two-thirds of members had signed on.

stories.title: Paytm's profit jumps 79% as India's payments giant turns the corner
stories.what_happened: On Monday, July 20, Paytm reported its April–June results: net profit jumped 79% from a year earlier to ₹220 crore, and revenue rose 28% to ₹2,448 crore, driven by its payments business. It was Paytm's fifth straight quarter of net profit, and the value of merchant transactions on its platform (its Gross Merchandise Value) climbed about 31%. The board chose not to issue bonus shares for now, saying it wants to keep reinvesting in growth.
key_points: Net profit rose 79% to ₹220 crore in Q1 FY27
Revenue up 28% to ₹2,448 crore; fifth straight profitable quarter
Merchant payments (GMV) grew about 31%
Board shelved a bonus-share issue to prioritise growth

depth.background: Paytm (run by One97 Communications) is one of India's best-known digital-payments apps, used to pay shops, transfer money and take small loans. After years of heavy losses and a 2024 regulatory blow to its banking arm, the company has been fighting to prove it can make sustained profits rather than just grow users. A 'quarter' is a three-month slice of the financial year; Q1 FY27 covers April–June 2026.

stories.title: India's oil import bill jumps ~60% to nearly $50 billion in three months — even as it buys less crude
stories.what_happened: Official data show India's crude oil import bill surged about 61% from a year earlier to $49.8 billion in the first quarter of FY27 (April-June 2026) — roughly 40% of what the country spent on crude in the entire previous year. Strikingly, the bill ballooned even though the volume of oil imported actually fell. The reasons: global prices have climbed on the US-Iran conflict and Strait of Hormuz risk, the discounts on Russian crude have shrunk, and the rupee has weakened toward record lows, making every dollar of oil more expensive in local terms.
key_points: April-June crude import bill up ~61% to $49.8 billion
That is already 40% of last year's entire crude bill
The bill rose even though import volumes actually fell
Fading war-era discounts and a weak rupee are the culprits

depth.background: India imports more than 85% of the crude oil it uses, so the global oil price and the rupee's exchange rate flow straight into its economy. For the past couple of years India cushioned itself by buying heavily discounted Russian crude, after Western sanctions on Moscow left Russia hunting for buyers. Those discounts, plus a relatively steady rupee, kept the import bill manageable.

stories.title: Rohit Sharma shuts down retirement talk — his focus stays on India and the 2027 World Cup
stories.what_happened: Speaking after the series, Rohit brushed the retirement talk aside, saying his sole focus remains on representing the country and contributing to the team — and reaffirming he plans to play the 2027 ODI World Cup, to be co-hosted by South Africa, Zimbabwe and Namibia. In the final ODI at Lord's on July 19 he made a fluent 138 off 110 balls, though India lost the match and the series 2-1. India's cricket board (BCCI) also publicly rejected reports that Lord's would be his last game, calling him a regular member of the ODI side.
key_points: Rohit dismisses speculation he's near the end of his ODI career
Says his sole focus is representing India and the team's success
Still targets the 2027 ODI World Cup in southern Africa
BCCI also rejects reports he'd retire after the Lord's ODI

depth.background: Rohit Sharma, 39, is one of India's greatest limited-overs batters and a former captain across formats. After a run of modest scores and India's loss of the ODI series in England, sections of the media suggested he could be nearing the end of his one-day international (ODI) career, with talk that the Lord's decider might be his last India appearance. The speculation grew loud enough that both Rohit and India's cricket board felt they had to respond.

thread.previously: Yesterday the series came down to a Lord's decider, with reports swirling that it could be Rohit Sharma's last India match and his future 'in the spotlight'.
thread.changed: Today Rohit himself ended the retirement talk — reaffirming his focus on India and the 2027 World Cup — and the BCCI publicly rejected the reports.

prev_ref.title: India vs England ODI series goes to a Lord's decider — with Rohit Sharma's future in the spotlight

stories.title: 'El Mayo' — the last old-guard boss of Mexico's Sinaloa Cartel — is sentenced to life in a US prison
stories.what_happened: On Monday, July 20, US District Judge Brian Cogan sentenced Zambada to life in prison in Brooklyn federal court. He had pleaded guilty in August 2025 — after prosecutors agreed not to seek the death penalty — to running a continuing criminal enterprise and racketeering, acknowledging responsibility for 85 underlying crimes. He admitted that from 1980 onward he and his cartel moved at least 1.5 million kilograms of cocaine, most of it into the United States, and he was also handed a roughly $15 billion forfeiture order.
key_points: Ismael 'El Mayo' Zambada got life in a Brooklyn federal court
He co-founded and ran the Sinaloa Cartel for over four decades
Admitted moving 1.5 million+ kg of cocaine, mostly to the US
Also hit with a reported $15 billion forfeiture order

depth.background: The Sinaloa Cartel is one of the world's most powerful drug-trafficking organisations, long led jointly by the flamboyant Joaquín 'El Chapo' Guzmán and the quieter, more strategic Ismael 'El Mayo' Zambada. El Chapo was convicted in the US in 2019 and is serving life at the Supermax prison. Zambada, seen as the cartel's real dealmaker and day-to-day operator, evaded capture for decades until 2024, when he says he was kidnapped in Mexico and flown against his will to Texas — a still-disputed account that has strained US–Mexico relations.

stories.title: A Russian missile hits a merchant ship leaving Odesa — four Indian sailors are killed as the war spreads onto civilian cargo vessels
stories.what_happened: On the evening of July 19, the Guinea-Bissau-flagged cargo vessel MV Golden Leo was struck by a Russian cruise missile as it departed Odesa, igniting a fire. Of 17 crew on board — five of them Indian — four Indian nationals were killed and one is hospitalised in critical condition; eight sailors were rescued. India's Ministry of External Affairs confirmed the deaths on July 20 and said its mission in Ukraine is assisting the families. The BBC reports that intensifying Russian strikes on the Odesa region have killed around 28 people in July alone.
key_points: Russian cruise missile struck the cargo ship MV Golden Leo off Odesa
Four Indian crew killed, one critical; eight rescued from 17 aboard
Russia's July strikes on the Odesa region have now killed ~28
The Black Sea is again a shooting gallery for civilian shipping

depth.background: Odesa is Ukraine's main Black Sea port and the outlet for much of the grain and cargo that leaves the country by sea. Since Russia walked away from a deal that had protected these shipping lanes, merchant ships loading there have increasingly been caught in Russian missile and drone attacks. The crews are mostly foreign sailors hired from countries like India and the Philippines, so an attack on a ship often kills citizens of nations with no part in the war.

stories.title: India's core industries grew 5% in June — a five-month high, and the first read on a revamped index
stories.what_happened: On July 20 the government released the June 2026 core-sector data under a brand-new series: the base year moves from 2011-12 to 2022-23, iron ore is added (taking the count from eight sectors to nine), and the weightages are revised. On this new basis, core output rose 5% year-on-year in June — a five-month high — led by iron ore (+43.9%), electricity (+9.8%) and cement (+9.8%). For the April–June quarter, core industries expanded 3.6%, up sharply from 1% a year earlier.
key_points: Eight (now nine) core industries grew 5% in June, a five-month high
Government relaunched the index with 2022-23 as the new base year
Iron ore added as a ninth sector; weightages revised
Iron ore +43.9%, electricity +9.8%, cement +9.8% led the gains

depth.background: The Index of Core Industries (ICI) tracks the output of India's foundational sectors — things like electricity, cement, steel, coal and refinery products. Because these feed almost everything else, the index is an early, closely watched signal of how the wider economy and factory activity are doing. Statisticians periodically refresh such indices with a newer 'base year' so the basket reflects today's economy rather than one from over a decade ago.

stories.title: Yemen's Houthis declare a naval blockade of Saudi Arabia — a new front opens in the widening Gulf war
stories.what_happened: On Monday, July 20, Houthi military spokesman Yahya Saree declared an immediate naval blockade of Saudi Arabia, saying it was a response to what the group calls Saudi Arabia's long-running 'siege' of Yemen. The announcement — reported by Reuters, AFP and Gulf outlets — is so far a declaration; the Houthis have not yet shown they can actually halt Saudi-bound shipping, and it lands amid the ongoing US-Iran conflict.
key_points: Houthi spokesman Yahya Saree announced an 'immediate' maritime blockade
The group says it answers a Saudi 'siege' of Yemen
Follows the first Houthi-Saudi fire exchange in years
Saudi Arabia is weighing a military response

depth.background: The Houthis are an Iran-aligned armed movement that controls northern Yemen, including the capital Sanaa, after years of civil war against a Saudi-led coalition. Since 2023 they have attacked ships in the Red Sea, one of the world's busiest trade routes. Tensions with Saudi Arabia flared again this month: the Houthis accuse Riyadh of striking Sanaa's airport to block a plane carrying their officials, and last week the two sides traded direct fire for the first time in years, with Houthi missiles aimed at the Saudi city of Abha.

stories.title: F1's new 2026 engines come under fire — Piastri says grids are being decided by 'computers behaving or misbehaving'
stories.what_happened: After the Belgian Grand Prix, McLaren's Oscar Piastri — one of the championship's front-runners — openly criticised the new formula. 'It sucks,' he said. 'When you've got qualifying grids decided by computers behaving or misbehaving, it's a pretty crap way of going racing.' At Spa, an 'energy-starved' circuit of long straights where cars struggle to recharge, Piastri lost big chunks of time to team-mate Lando Norris despite identical settings, because his engine mismanaged its energy. Mercedes' George Russell hit the same wall — short of battery, he was left, in his words, 'slower than a Formula 2 car' on the run to Les Combes, where he collided with Hamilton and retired.
key_points: Piastri: 'a pretty crap way of going racing' — outcomes hinge on software
New 2026 power units self-learn energy use, but are hyper-sensitive
At 'energy-starved' Spa, cars ran flat on the straights
Russell was left 'slower than a Formula 2 car' off the line

depth.background: 2026 is Formula 1's biggest rules reset in a generation. The new power units run on a near 50/50 split between the petrol engine and electric power, so managing the battery — 'harvesting' energy under braking and 'deploying' it on the straights — now decides lap time as much as raw engine power. To handle this, the engines use self-learning software that adapts lap by lap, even within a lap, to predict how to spend the battery. The catch: the system is extremely sensitive, so tiny changes in a driver's inputs, the grip, or the wind can throw its calculations off — and the driver can't fully control it.

stories.title: India says its capital spending has jumped five-fold to ₹90.87 lakh crore over 2014-26 — the engine behind its growth story
stories.what_happened: On Monday, July 20, the Finance Ministry told Parliament that the Centre's 'effective' capital expenditure reached ₹90.87 lakh crore during 2014-26 — more than five times the ₹17.04 lakh crore spent in the previous decade (2004-14). Of that, ₹64.70 lakh crore was the Centre's own direct spending and ₹26.17 lakh crore was grants to states to build assets. Capex as a share of GDP rose from 1.6% in 2014-15 to 3.2% in 2023-24. ('Effective capex' bundles the Centre's own spending with the grants it gives states to build.)
key_points: Effective capex ₹90.87 lakh crore in 2014-26 vs ₹17.04 lakh crore in 2004-14
The Centre's own direct capex rose to ₹64.70 lakh crore
₹26.17 lakh crore of that went as grants to states
Capex as a share of GDP doubled from 1.6% to 3.2%

depth.background: 'Capital expenditure' (capex) is money a government spends building things that last — roads, railways, ports, power lines — as opposed to day-to-day running costs or welfare handouts. Since around 2020, New Delhi has leaned hard on public capex as its main tool to drive growth, betting that building infrastructure creates jobs now and makes the whole economy more productive later. These figures were tabled in Parliament as the monsoon session got under way.

stories.title: HDFC Bank delays its CEO's reappointment as independent directors probe deposit-rate claims
stories.what_happened: Reuters reported on July 20 that HDFC Bank's board has not yet recommended Jagdishan's reappointment to the RBI, because its independent directors are still finishing an additional review into media reports alleging the bank offered preferential interest rates on certain large deposits. His term ends in October, and the findings are expected in early August. Deputy Managing Director Kaizad Bharucha said on July 19 that the board is 'seized' of the matter and will decide soon; two of three sources said the review has so far found no evidence of wrongdoing.
key_points: HDFC Bank hasn't yet asked the RBI to reappoint CEO Jagdishan
Independent directors are reviewing preferential-deposit-rate allegations
His current term ends in October; findings expected early August
Two of three sources say no wrongdoing has surfaced so far

depth.background: HDFC Bank is India's largest private-sector bank and one of the biggest weights on the stock market, so who runs it matters to millions of investors. Sashidhar Jagdishan has been its managing director and CEO since 2020. In India, a bank cannot simply reappoint its own boss — the choice must be approved by the Reserve Bank of India (RBI), the country's banking regulator and central bank. The bank recently brought in a new part-time chairman, Gyanesh Kumar, a former Chief Election Commissioner.

stories.title: Dozens feared dead after an overloaded ferry sinks off Guyana — 67 rescued, but no survivors found since
stories.what_happened: The ferry capsized about seven miles from shore late on Saturday, after a distress call around 11 p.m. Authorities believe roughly 133 people were on board — well above what the official passenger list showed. Sixty-seven people were rescued, including 15 children, but no new survivors have been found since Sunday afternoon, and dozens remain missing and feared dead. Officials say the manifest was inaccurate and are investigating possible crew drug use as a factor; the captain and other crew members have been detained.
key_points: MV Barima capsized about 7 miles offshore late Saturday
Roughly 133 people aboard; 67 rescued, including 15 children
The passenger manifest was inaccurate, hampering the search
Captain and crew detained; possible crew drug use under probe

depth.background: Guyana, a small country on the northern coast of South America, relies heavily on river and coastal ferries to connect remote communities that roads do not reach. The MV Barima was making a routine run from the capital, Georgetown, to the mining village of Port Kaituma when it went down — a lifeline route where boats are often crowded.

stories.title: Hamas picks Khalil al-Hayya as its new leader — its lead ceasefire negotiator now heads the group
stories.what_happened: On Monday, July 20, Hamas said al-Hayya had been elected head of its political bureau (its top decision-making body) after an internal ballot, beating former leader Khaled Meshaal in a run-off by a large majority. Hamas's military wing, the Ezzedine al-Qassam Brigades, publicly backed him. He is expected to formally assume the role within the week.
key_points: Al-Hayya won an internal run-off against veteran Khaled Meshaal
He is Hamas's chief negotiator in the Israel ceasefire talks
Armed wing, the Qassam Brigades, declared 'full support'
He is expected to formally take office next week

depth.background: Hamas is the armed Palestinian group that has run Gaza since 2007 and is at war with Israel. Its top leader, Yahya Sinwar, was killed by Israeli forces in October 2024, and since then the group has been run by a committee rather than one figure. Khalil al-Hayya, a Gaza-born veteran of the movement, has been its most public face abroad — the man leading the on-and-off ceasefire and hostage talks brokered by Qatar and Egypt.

stories.title: The EU hits AliExpress with a record €550m fine for letting unsafe and fake goods flood its site
stories.what_happened: On Monday, July 20, the European Commission — the EU's executive and top regulator — fined AliExpress a record €550 million (about £470m / $630m) for failing to stop the sale of illegal and unsafe goods, including counterfeit clothing, dangerous toys, and harmful cosmetics and kitchen gadgets. It is the largest penalty issued so far under the DSA. AliExpress said it was 'shocked' by the size of the fine and can challenge it.
key_points: EU fines AliExpress €550m (about $630m) — its biggest such penalty
Regulators say it failed to stop unsafe toys and counterfeit goods
Largest fine yet under the EU's new Digital Services Act
AliExpress says it is 'shocked'; it can appeal

depth.background: AliExpress is a giant online marketplace owned by China's Alibaba, where independent sellers list cheap products shipped worldwide. In 2022 the EU passed the Digital Services Act (DSA) — a landmark law that makes big online platforms legally responsible for policing illegal and dangerous content and products on their sites. Regulators had warned AliExpress to fix how it screens sellers; this fine says it didn't do enough.

stories.title: After losing the England series, captain Gill sounds the alarm on India's fitness: 'we're missing a trick'
stories.what_happened: After the Lord's defeat, Gill publicly questioned the team's fitness, saying a 'minimum five players did not play this match' from the squad originally announced, and that 'if a player is missing after every match, somewhere we are missing a trick.' He said the group — players and the BCCI's medical staff together — must focus on improving fitness, with the 2027 50-over World Cup approaching.
key_points: India lost the ODI series 2-1 at Lord's
At least five first-choice players missed the decider injured
Star pacer Bumrah played just 2 of 3 ODIs before being ruled out
Gill flags fitness with the 2027 World Cup looming

depth.background: India have just lost a three-match one-day international (ODI) series in England 2-1, with the decider at Lord's. Across the tour the squad was battered by injuries — Hardik Pandya, Nitish Kumar Reddy, Harshit Rana, Washington Sundar and star fast bowler Jasprit Bumrah all missing games. Shubman Gill is India's captain.

stories.title: Congo's Ebola outbreak becomes the fastest-growing on record — at least 930 dead, spreading faster than trackers can follow
