stories.what_happened: Officials say at least 930 people have died out of 2,344 confirmed cases, with 37 deaths recorded in a single 24-hour stretch from Friday into Saturday — one of the highest daily totals yet. The World Health Organization (WHO) calls it the fastest-growing Ebola outbreak on record. It is caused by the Bundibugyo virus, a less common Ebola strain for which there is no approved vaccine or treatment — the existing Ebola vaccine targets a different strain.
key_points: At least 930 dead, 2,344 confirmed cases since mid-May
Caused by the rarer Bundibugyo strain — no approved vaccine or cure
80% of new cases from unknown chains — spreading unseen
Rebel violence and unpaid, walked-off health workers cripple the response

depth.background: Ebola is a severe, often-fatal viral disease — high fever, and in bad cases internal bleeding — spread through contact with an infected person's bodily fluids. The Democratic Republic of Congo (DRC), in central Africa, has battled repeated outbreaks. This one was declared on May 15, 2026, in the country's east, a region where armed rebel groups operate, which makes health work dangerous.

stories.title: Meta goes on trial in Tennessee over claims Instagram was built to hook kids — the first of several 'addiction' cases
stories.what_happened: Jury selection opened on Monday, July 20, in Nashville, in the first phase of a trial expected to run about seven weeks. Tennessee must convince the jury that Meta violated state law by knowingly building an addictive product and misleading the public about its safety. If the state wins, a second phase decides financial penalties and possible court-ordered changes to Instagram. Meta denies the claims, arguing it works to protect children and should not be liable for content its users post.
key_points: Jury selection began Monday in Nashville
Tennessee says Meta hid research showing Instagram harms teens
Roughly seven-week trial; overlaps two more Meta cases in California
A New Mexico jury already found Meta misled users, awarded $375M

depth.background: Meta owns Instagram, Facebook and WhatsApp. For years, US states, parents and school districts have alleged the company deliberately designed features — endless scrolling feeds, constant notifications, 'likes' — to make young users compulsively return, while playing down its own internal research on harm. Tennessee's Attorney General, Jonathan Skrmetti, sued under the state's consumer-protection law.

stories.title: Mercedes has F1's fastest engine — and keeps beating itself: Wolff admits reliability is now a title threat as Verstappen rumours swirl
stories.what_happened: Speaking after Sunday's Belgian Grand Prix at Spa — where Russell retired from second place — Wolff conceded, 'We have the most powerful power unit, a strong car that is capable of winning,' but admitted 'we lost a lot of points to reliability.' Electrical gremlins had already knocked Antonelli out at Barcelona and Russell out of the lead in Canada. Even so, Mercedes has won seven of nine races, and its constructors'-championship lead over Ferrari has shrunk to 73 points.
key_points: Wolff: 'We have the most powerful power unit'
But electrical failures cost wins at Barcelona, Canada and now Spa
Russell led in Canada and ran 2nd at Spa before breakdowns
Reliability doubts fuel talk of Verstappen joining Mercedes

depth.background: Formula 1 introduced all-new engine rules for 2026, and Mercedes appears to have built the strongest 'power unit' (the hybrid engine). But raw speed counts for nothing if the car doesn't finish — and Mercedes has repeatedly broken down while leading. Team principal Toto Wolff runs the operation; George Russell and rookie Kimi Antonelli are its drivers, with Antonelli now leading the drivers' championship.

stories.title: West Bengal's new BJP government makes Bengali compulsory for all official work from September 1
stories.what_happened: On July 20, the West Bengal government announced that from September 1, Bengali will be mandatory for all official government work — files, notifications, and communication at police stations — and that correspondence with the central government will be done in Bengali alongside Hindi. Officials will spend roughly the next six weeks translating necessary documents. The move fulfils a manifesto promise to preserve Bengali heritage and promote the language.
key_points: Bengali mandatory in state files, notices and police stations
Even letters to the Centre to be in Bengali (alongside Hindi)
First BJP CM Suvendu Adhikari fulfilling an election promise
About six weeks set aside to translate documents

depth.background: West Bengal, an eastern state of roughly 100 million people, is fiercely proud of its Bengali language and culture. In the 2026 assembly elections the BJP ended Mamata Banerjee's Trinamool Congress's long rule, and Suvendu Adhikari became the state's first-ever BJP chief minister in May 2026 — from a party rivals often accuse of favouring Hindi over regional tongues.

stories.title: Deadly monsoon floods hammer India's north and northeast — J&K toll climbs to 18 as the Army launches 'Operation Jal Rahat' in Assam
stories.what_happened: Torrential rain and cloudburst-triggered flash floods struck Jammu & Kashmir's border districts of Poonch and Rajouri on July 19; the death toll has since risen to 18 (about 15 in Poonch, 3 in Rajouri), with over 20 houses washed away and people still missing. In Assam, floods across seven districts have affected more than 57,000 people, prompting the Army and Assam Rifles to launch 'Operation Jal Rahat' ('Operation Water Relief'); the Air Force is on standby and rail services are disrupted. The weather office (IMD) issued a red alert for parts of Himachal Pradesh.
key_points: Jammu & Kashmir: at least 18 dead in Poonch and Rajouri
Cloudbursts washed away 20+ homes; several still missing
Army's 'Operation Jal Rahat' aids 57,000+ hit in Assam
IMD red alert for Himachal; rail traffic disrupted

depth.background: India's monsoon (June–September) delivers most of the year's rain — vital for farming, but capable of deadly flooding and landslides, especially in the Himalayan north and the low-lying northeast. 'Cloudbursts' — sudden, extremely intense downpours — can turn hill streams into torrents within minutes.

thread.previously: Yesterday we reported cloudburst landslides in Nagaland and early flash floods in Kashmir as the monsoon turned violent across India's north and northeast.
thread.changed: Today the Kashmir toll has climbed to 18 in Poonch and Rajouri, and the Army has launched 'Operation Jal Rahat' in Assam, where floods now affect 57,000+ people, with a red alert issued for Himachal Pradesh.

prev_ref.title: Monsoon fury batters India's north and northeast — a deadly cloudburst landslide in Nagaland, flash floods in Kashmir

stories.title: Government tells Parliament it has no plan to scrap the LTCG tax on shares — ending months of investor rumours
stories.what_happened: On Monday, July 20, Minister of State for Finance Pankaj Chaudhary told the Lok Sabha (Parliament's lower house) that there is 'no proposal under consideration' to abolish LTCG tax on listed equities for retail and domestic investors. He added that capital-gains rates are reviewed only periodically, during the annual Budget, in light of the wider economy.
key_points: Minister Pankaj Chaudhary: 'no proposal under consideration' to abolish it
LTCG on listed shares held over a year is taxed at 12.5%
Gains up to ₹1.25 lakh a year stay tax-free
Any change would come only in the annual Budget

depth.background: 'Long-term capital gains' (LTCG) tax is the tax you pay on the profit when you sell an investment you've held for a while. For listed Indian shares and equity mutual funds held more than a year, profits above ₹1.25 lakh in a year are taxed at 12.5%. Investors have long lobbied to scrap it, arguing it discourages long-term investing — and speculation had grown louder after the government recently gave foreign investors a tax break on government bonds, fuelling talk that domestic equity taxes might be eased too.

stories.title: Tripura's police chief is found dead in his office — police suspect he shot himself with his service pistol
stories.what_happened: On Monday, July 20, Dhankar was found dead inside his office at the Tripura police headquarters in Agartala. Preliminary indications, according to police, are that he may have died by suicide using his service pistol, though an investigation has been launched to establish exactly what happened. His body was moved to GB Pant Hospital in Agartala for a post-mortem examination.
key_points: DGP Anurag Dhankar was found dead at the state police headquarters in Agartala
Early findings point to suicide with his own service pistol; a probe is on
A 1994-batch IPS officer, he became Tripura's police chief in May 2025
At the CBI he had led the probe into the Nirav Modi bank-fraud case

depth.background: The Director General of Police (DGP) is the top uniformed officer in an Indian state — the person who runs its entire police force. Anurag Dhankar was a 1994-batch officer of the Indian Police Service (IPS), the elite all-India cadre that staffs senior police posts. He had a high-profile career, including a long stint at the Central Bureau of Investigation (CBI), India's top federal investigating agency, where he led the investigation into fugitive diamond merchant Nirav Modi's alleged multi-crore bank fraud.

stories.title: India's 'yo-yo' monsoon — drought, then deluge, then drought — threatens crops and the power grid as a 'super' El Niño builds
stories.what_happened: Analysts and a report by the Centre for Research on Energy and Clean Air (CREA) warn that an intensifying El Niño, amplified by global warming, is making India's 2026 monsoon lurch between extremes. The season opened with the fifth-driest June on record — rain almost 40% below normal — then dumped roughly a month's rain on cities like Mumbai in a single week, before stalling into another dry break that left national rainfall about 24% below normal by July 19. Scientists say the El Niño could be among the strongest on record, peaking between late 2026 and early 2027.
key_points: Fifth-driest June on record (~40% below normal), then a sudden deluge
Now stalled again — rainfall ~24% below normal as of July 19
Erratic rain hits rain-fed crops: rice, pulses, oilseeds, cotton, sugar
A strong El Niño could stress India's power grid more than any nation's

depth.background: About half of India's farmland has no irrigation and depends entirely on the June–September monsoon rains, and those rains also refill the reservoirs that run hydro power and cool thermal plants. El Niño is a periodic warming of the Pacific Ocean that tends to weaken India's monsoon and push up temperatures. When the rains arrive in erratic bursts instead of a steady spread, both farming and electricity get squeezed at once.

stories.title: Modi opens Parliament by touting 7.7% growth 'despite' the Middle East war — India's economy holds up even as oil bites
stories.what_happened: On Monday, July 20, addressing the media as Parliament's monsoon session opened, Prime Minister Narendra Modi said India had maintained a 7.7% growth rate and remained the world's fastest-growing major economy, calling it a reflection of the country's 'inherent strength.' He explicitly tied the achievement to the strain of the Middle East war, noting India depends on others for petrol, diesel, LPG, fertilisers and chemicals, and urged all MPs to make the session productive.
key_points: PM Modi: India still grew 7.7%, the fastest of any big economy
He framed it as strength 'despite' the West Asia (Iran–US) war
That war has pushed oil past $90 — costly for import-reliant India
Said at the chaotic first day of Parliament's monsoon session

depth.background: India buys most of its crude oil, cooking gas, fertiliser and many chemicals from abroad — so when war in the Middle East (which the government calls 'West Asia') pushes energy prices up, India's import bill swells and prices at home can rise. GDP (Gross Domestic Product) is the total value of everything a country produces in a year; its growth rate is the headline measure of how fast the economy is expanding. India has been the fastest-growing large economy for a few years, and the government leans on that number as proof its policies are working.

stories.title: FBI agents told to stop investigating ICE — the main outside check on immigration-agent violence is being pulled back
stories.what_happened: The New York Times reported that FBI agents have been told they will no longer investigate confrontations involving ICE officers; those cases will instead go to Homeland Security Investigations (HSI), which sits inside the same department as ICE. The Verge, CBS News and CNBC carried the same reporting. The Departments of Homeland Security and Justice denied to the Times that any policy had changed. It follows renewed scrutiny after ICE agents killed two people in recent weeks, including a 52-year-old Mexican man, Lorenzo Salgado Araujo, in Houston.
key_points: NYT: FBI staff told to drop cases involving ICE agents
Such cases move to ICE's own parent agency, HSI
Follows two people killed by ICE agents in recent weeks
DHS and DOJ deny any policy change

depth.background: ICE (Immigration and Customs Enforcement) is the federal agency that arrests and deports people in the US; it has been carrying out a huge deportation drive, and confrontations between its agents and the public have turned deadly. Until now, when an ICE agent was accused of a crime — say, an unjustified shooting — the FBI, a separate agency, could investigate independently. That independence is what stops an agency from policing itself.

stories.title: Parliament's monsoon session opens in chaos — both Houses adjourned as the Opposition storms over exam-paper leaks
stories.what_happened: On the session's first day, Monday, July 20, both Houses were adjourned shortly after convening as Opposition members protested and demanded a formal discussion on the paper leaks and the student agitation. Prime Minister Narendra Modi, in his customary pre-session address, said he hoped 'both the monsoon and the Monsoon Session are productive' and praised young Indian innovators — while taking a veiled swipe at a '56-year-old youth', widely read as a jab at Opposition leader Rahul Gandhi. Outside Parliament, police lathi-charged (baton-charged) and detained members of the 'Chalo Sansad' (Let's go to Parliament) march near Jantar Mantar, where prohibitory orders were in force.
key_points: Lok Sabha and Rajya Sabha both adjourned soon after opening
Opposition demands a debate on question-paper leaks and student protests
Outside, police lathi-charged 'Cockroach' (CJP) marchers near Parliament
PM Modi appealed for 'a productive session'; it runs until August 13

depth.background: India's Parliament has two chambers — the Lok Sabha (lower house, directly elected) and the Rajya Sabha (upper house). It meets in fixed 'sessions', and the monsoon session is the mid-year one. For weeks, anger has built over leaked exam question papers — recruitment and entrance tests whose answers escaped in advance, wrecking the chances of honest students — and a linked youth protest movement (the 'Cockroach'/CJP campaign) demanding accountability. The Opposition had signalled it would use the session to corner the government and demand Education Minister Dharmendra Pradhan's resignation.

thread.previously: Yesterday we said the Opposition would use the monsoon session's opening to demand Education Minister Dharmendra Pradhan's resignation over the exam-leak scandal, with a youth 'Cockroach' march planned for Parliament.
thread.changed: Today it played out: both Houses were adjourned amid Opposition uproar on day one, and police lathi-charged and detained marchers outside Parliament.

prev_ref.title: Exam-leak anger reaches Parliament: opposition will demand Education Minister Dharmendra Pradhan quit as the monsoon session opens tomorrow

stories.title: 'Cockroach' march to Parliament turns violent — police baton-charge protesters in Delhi
stories.what_happened: On Monday, July 20, thousands of CJP supporters tried to march from Jantar Mantar toward Parliament. Police, who had shut mobile internet in parts of central Delhi and deployed heavy barricades, fired tear-gas shells and baton-charged ('lathi-charged') the crowd near Parliament Street and Shastri Bhawan after protesters allegedly tried to breach the barricades. Several police officers and protesters were injured and many demonstrators were detained. CJP founder Abhijeet Dipke ended his hunger strike, and party representatives went to meet Union Minister J.P. Nadda; Wangchuk, kept in hospital by a court order, was represented by his wife Gitanjali Angmo.
key_points: CJP's 'Sansad Chalo' march reached central Delhi
Police used tear gas and baton-charges near Parliament Street
Officers and protesters injured; several detained
Founder Abhijeet Dipke ended his hunger strike

depth.background: The Cockroach Janta Party (CJP) is a new Gen-Z-led movement born out of anger over repeated leaks of India's public exams — the tests millions of young people take for government jobs and college places. It had called a 'Sansad Chalo' ('March to Parliament') for July 20, the opening day of Parliament's monsoon session. Delhi Police had already declared the march unauthorised and invoked BNSS Section 163 (a legal order that bans large gatherings), while activist Sonam Wangchuk and CJP figures backed the cause.

thread.previously: Delhi Police had declared the CJP's July 20 march to Parliament illegal, saying no permission was granted, while a hospitalised Sonam Wangchuk refused to break his fast.
thread.changed: The march went ahead anyway: police fired tear gas and baton-charged protesters near Parliament Street, injuring officers and demonstrators, and founder Abhijeet Dipke ended his hunger strike as talks with the government began.

prev_ref.title: Delhi Police declare the 'Cockroach' party's July 20 march to Parliament illegal as a hospitalised Wangchuk refuses to break his fast

stories.title: India's IT giants pull back on fresh graduates — delayed joining dates, quietly withdrawn offers, and a salary-hike rethink
stories.what_happened: Reports say IT companies are revisiting their campus-hiring playbook: joining dates for graduates who already hold offers are being pushed back, some roles are being quietly withdrawn, and start dates are being tied to shifting business demand. Separately, Accenture overhauled its salary-hike structure — reworking how raises are handed out after disappointing results. The common thread: firms are hiring fewer generalist freshers and leaning toward specialised, AI-ready skills.
key_points: IT firms delaying joining dates and withdrawing campus offers
Accenture overhauls salary hikes after weak results
Hiring shifts toward specialised, AI-ready skills
Freshers left waiting with uncertain start dates

depth.background: India's roughly $250-billion IT services industry — TCS, Infosys, Wipro, and global players like Accenture — has for decades been the country's great white-collar job engine, hiring hundreds of thousands of engineering graduates a year. But two forces are squeezing it: soft demand from Western clients, and AI tools that can now do chunks of the entry-level coding and support work freshers used to be hired for. A weak set of quarterly results earlier this month (the 'IT bloodbath') already spooked the sector.

stories.title: Oil jumps back above $90 as Iran chokes the Strait of Hormuz
stories.what_happened: On Monday, July 20, Brent crude climbed about 2% to more than $90 a barrel — its highest since mid-June — after Iran's Revolutionary Guard said two oil tankers had been 'blown up and immobilised' overnight while trying to cross what it called an 'unsafe' southern route through the Strait of Hormuz. Iran vowed that 'not a single drop' of oil or gas would pass the strait. Traffic collapsed: only four vessels transited on Sunday, down from eight the day before. The US said it hit Iranian military and coastal-surveillance sites for a ninth straight night, partly to protect shipping.
key_points: Brent crude rose ~2% past $90 a barrel
Iran says it blew up two tankers in the strait
Only 4 ships crossed Sunday, down from 8
US carried out a ninth night of strikes on Iran

depth.background: The Strait of Hormuz is a narrow sea passage between Iran and Oman through which roughly a fifth of the world's oil ships every day — the single most important oil chokepoint on Earth. For nine days the US and Iran have been trading direct military blows, and Iran has repeatedly warned it could disrupt shipping there. 'Brent crude' is the global benchmark price for a barrel of oil; when it rises, fuel and shipping costs rise worldwide.

stories.title: Ukraine launches its largest drone attack on Moscow in two years — an oil depot and a big warehouse go up in flames
stories.what_happened: Overnight into Monday, July 20, Ukraine launched what Moscow says was more than 400 drones toward the Russian capital region — the largest such assault on Moscow in roughly two years. Mayor Sergey Sobyanin said about 85 drones were shot down as they neared the city. An oil depot and a fulfilment warehouse of online retailer Wildberries caught fire near Podolsk, a town about 40km (25 miles) south of the Kremlin, and regional governor Andrei Vorobyov said homes and infrastructure were damaged in the Podolsk, Domodedovo and Odintsovo districts. At least 10 people were hurt, including a child.
key_points: Moscow's mayor says 400+ Ukrainian drones were aimed at the capital region overnight
An oil depot and a Wildberries warehouse near Podolsk, ~40km south of the Kremlin, caught fire
At least 10 people wounded, including a child; homes damaged in several districts
It's the biggest drone raid on Moscow in about two years; Zelensky vows more

depth.background: Russia invaded Ukraine in February 2022, and for most of the war the destruction has flowed one way — Russian missiles and drones pounding Ukrainian cities. Over the past year Ukraine has increasingly hit back with cheap, long-range attack drones (small pilotless aircraft packed with explosives) aimed at Russian oil refineries, fuel depots and military sites, trying to raise the cost of the war for ordinary Russians and choke the fuel that funds Moscow's army.

stories.title: Lockheed unveils a cheaper Patriot missile — a scramble to fix air defense's 'cost problem' as drones overwhelm the world's shields
stories.what_happened: On Monday, July 20, Lockheed Martin announced a lower-cost interceptor, the PAC-3 Adapted Capability Effector (ACE), which it says will cost less than half as much as the ~$4 million PAC-3 MSE while still working with the Patriot system. The company said initial production could begin within about 36 months and that it plans to develop and build the missile with US and European industry partners. The move responds to soaring global demand and to competition from startups making cheaper weapons.
key_points: New PAC-3 ACE interceptor to cost under half of the ~$4M PAC-3 MSE
Meant to down cheap drones without spending millions per shot
Demand soaring from Ukraine, the Iran–US war and Gulf instability
Production could start within ~36 months, with US and European partners

depth.background: A Patriot is a US-made air-defense system that fires interceptor missiles to knock incoming missiles and drones out of the sky; it has become one of the world's most sought-after weapons since Russia's 2022 invasion of Ukraine. But its top interceptor, the PAC-3 MSE, costs roughly $4 million each — a punishing price when enemies attack with drones that can cost as little as tens of thousands of dollars. That mismatch, known as the 'cost-exchange problem', means defenders can win every intercept and still lose the war of money. Meanwhile, Silicon Valley-backed startups are pitching cheaper weapons made at scale, pressuring the big traditional contractors.

stories.title: India clears its first-ever dengue vaccine — Takeda's Qdenga gets the regulator's nod
stories.what_happened: On July 20, the Drug Controller General of India (DCGI) — the country's top drug regulator — approved Qdenga, a dengue vaccine made by Japanese drugmaker Takeda, making it the first dengue vaccine cleared for use in India. It is given as two shots three months apart, for people aged 4 to 60, and works against all four dengue serotypes. Unlike an earlier dengue vaccine, Qdenga does not require testing whether a person was previously infected before vaccination. In its large Phase 3 trial, it showed about 80% efficacy against lab-confirmed dengue one year after the second dose and roughly 84-90% protection against dengue hospitalization over several years. Qdenga is already approved in more than 40 countries, with over 32 million doses distributed.
key_points: India's drug regulator (DCGI) approves Qdenga, the country's first dengue vaccine
Two doses, three months apart, for people aged 4 to 60
Protects against all four dengue virus types; no prior-infection test needed
Trials showed ~80% protection against dengue a year after the second dose

depth.background: Dengue is a mosquito-borne viral illness that surges across India every monsoon, causing high fever, body aches and, in severe cases, dangerous drops in blood platelets that can be fatal. It comes in four closely related types (serotypes), and catching one type can make a later infection with another type worse — which is exactly why a safe dengue vaccine has been so hard to develop. Until now India had no approved vaccine and relied on mosquito control and hospital care.

stories.title: Ryanair's profit falls by a third — the first big company to show what the Iran war is doing to real businesses
stories.what_happened: On Monday, July 20, Ryanair reported that profit for its April–June quarter fell 34% to €538 million (about $615 million), down from €820 million a year earlier — missing forecasts. Operating costs rose 11% as the price of the roughly 20% of its fuel that was not hedged more than doubled to around £111 a barrel. At the same time, average fares fell 6% because the Middle East conflict, worries about EU jet-fuel shortages and general uncertainty made travellers hesitant and slower to book. Ryanair's shares fell about 6%, and CEO Michael O'Leary said the airline has 'zero visibility' for the second half of its year.
key_points: Quarterly profit drops 34% to €538 million
Jet-fuel prices doubled as the Iran war sent oil soaring
Ryanair cut fares 6% as nervous travellers booked less
It can't give full-year guidance — 'zero visibility' for winter

depth.background: Ryanair is Europe's biggest low-cost airline, known for cheap fares and packed planes. Airlines are hugely exposed to the price of jet fuel, which is refined from crude oil — so when oil prices jump, an airline's costs jump with them unless it has 'hedged' (locked in prices in advance). The ongoing US–Iran war has pushed oil back above $90 a barrel and rattled travel demand.

stories.title: Sensex sinks 700 points as HDFC Bank's shrinking margins and pricey oil hit Dalal Street
stories.what_happened: On Monday, July 20, the Sensex dropped as much as 706 points (about 0.9%) to an intraday low around 77,445, while the Nifty 50 fell about 184 points to roughly 24,150. The trigger was HDFC Bank: its June-quarter profit was overshadowed by a sharper-than-expected fall in net interest margins, and the stock slid up to 5%, dragging Axis Bank, Kotak and the wider banking index down as brokerages flagged softer margins sector-wide. On top of that, Brent crude climbing past $90 weakened the rupee to a two-month low of 96.42 per dollar and spurred foreign investors to pull money out.
key_points: Sensex fell as much as 706 points; Nifty near 24,150
HDFC Bank slid up to 5% on weaker margins
Rupee slipped to a two-month low of 96.42/$
Brent crude topping $90 added to the pressure

depth.background: The Sensex and Nifty 50 are India's two main stock-market indexes — baskets of big companies whose combined price shows how the market is doing. Banks carry heavy weight in both, so when a giant like HDFC Bank (India's largest private lender) moves, the whole index moves. A bank's 'net interest margin' (NIM) is the gap between what it earns on loans and what it pays on deposits — its core profitability. Markets had also been jittery for days over the US-Iran conflict pushing up oil.

stories.title: India's switch to E20 petrol sparks a driver backlash — clogged injectors, lower mileage and a political row
stories.what_happened: As E20 has rolled out, vehicle owners across the country have complained of lower fuel efficiency, clogged injectors and filters, swollen or cracked rubber parts, and worries about long-term engine wear — problems reported mostly in pre-2023 vehicles not designed for higher ethanol blends. Road Transport Minister Nitin Gadkari has dismissed the concerns, saying E20 was introduced only after extensive testing and that the government has received no verified complaints of engine damage. The row deepened after the government's own attorney general told the Supreme Court in June that the Centre was 'experimenting' with the blend — remarks the government later said were misunderstood.
key_points: Petrol pumps now sell E20 — fuel with 20% ethanol blended in — almost everywhere
Owners report lower mileage, clogged filters and fuel-system trouble, mostly in older cars
The government moved its 20% blending target forward from 2030 to 2025
Minister Nitin Gadkari says testing shows no engine damage; critics disagree

depth.background: Ethanol is an alcohol-based fuel made from crops like sugarcane; blending it into petrol is meant to cut India's costly oil imports, support farmers, and lower some emissions. 'E20' means the petrol is 20% ethanol, 80% regular petrol. Under its National Policy on Biofuels, the Modi government pulled its 20%-blending goal forward by five years — from 2030 to 2025 — and E20 is now the standard at pumps nationwide.

stories.title: The rupee is inching toward a record low as costlier oil eats into India's finances
stories.what_happened: In early trade on Monday, July 20, the rupee fell about 12 paise to roughly 96.42 against the US dollar, leaving it within striking distance of its record low. Traders blame a fresh surge in oil prices — Brent crude is back above $90 a barrel — driven by the escalating US–Iran war that has choked the Strait of Hormuz, the narrow sea lane through which much of the world's oil moves. The currency has now dropped about 1.7% this month.
key_points: Rupee slips to about 96.42 per dollar, near its all-time low of 96.96
The Iran war has pushed oil back above $90 a barrel
India imports most of its oil, so a weak rupee makes it pricier still
A cheaper rupee feeds inflation and raises the import bill

depth.background: The rupee is India's currency, and its value is usually measured against the US dollar. India buys more than 80% of the oil it uses from abroad, and that oil is priced in dollars — so when oil gets expensive OR the rupee gets weaker, India's fuel bill jumps on both counts. The rupee has been sliding for weeks and touched an all-time low of 96.96 per dollar back in May.

stories.title: A cheaper-AI scare hits global tech stocks — IBM suffers its worst day since 1987, Asian markets wobble
stories.what_happened: IBM's stock plunged roughly 25% in a single session — its steepest one-day drop since the 1987 'Black Monday' crash — after the company warned that the AI boom is squeezing corporate software budgets, meaning clients are diverting money into AI and away from IBM's products. The selloff spread: on Monday, July 20, Asian markets were mixed-to-lower as investors 'reassessed AI bets', with South Korea's market sliding while China outperformed. Separately, Apple has overtaken Nvidia as the world's most valuable company (about $4.88 trillion) as money rotates away from the AI-chip leader.
key_points: IBM shares crashed ~25% — the biggest one-day fall since Black Monday 1987
Trigger: IBM warned the AI boom is squeezing company software budgets
Asian markets slid Monday as investors 'reassess AI bets'; South Korea fell
The fear: cheap AI models could hurt, not help, some tech incumbents

depth.background: For two years, investors have poured money into anything linked to artificial intelligence, assuming the boom lifts all tech companies. But cheaper, freely available AI models — many from Chinese labs — are challenging that assumption: if powerful AI becomes cheap, companies may spend less on traditional software and expensive chips, and some established tech giants could be losers rather than winners. IBM, a century-old technology company, is one investors now worry is on the wrong side of that shift.

stories.title: Andy Burnham becomes UK prime minister, promising to 'rewire Britain' — the fifth PM in four years
stories.what_happened: Burnham was confirmed as Labour leader at a special party conference and is being formally installed as prime minister on Monday, July 20, succeeding Starmer. He has pledged to 'rewire Britain' with a distinctly leftwing but 'pro-business' programme: temporarily nationalising the troubled water company Thames Water (putting it into public hands), offering fresh support to North Sea oil and gas, and shifting money and decision-making toward the North. He has also floated scrapping the government's science-and-technology department.
key_points: Ex-Manchester mayor takes over from Keir Starmer as Labour leader and PM
A leftwing agenda: nationalise Thames Water, back North Sea oil, tilt power northward
Britain's fifth prime minister in four years — a run of political churn
Business is nervous; Thames Water's lenders threaten a legal fight

depth.background: In the UK, the prime minister is the leader of whichever party controls Parliament, so a party can change its leader — and thus the PM — without a national election. Labour has governed since 2024 under Keir Starmer, but after a slump in the party's fortunes it chose Andy Burnham, the popular mayor of Greater Manchester and a former health secretary, as its new leader. Burnham built his brand as the 'King of the North', championing England's less-wealthy northern regions against a London-centric establishment.

stories.title: A cash-theft scandal at the Ram temple reaches the Supreme Court — investigators report 8 arrests and 70 CCTV catches
stories.what_happened: On Monday, July 20, a Special Investigation Team (SIT) set up by the Uttar Pradesh government filed a status report in a sealed cover before a three-judge Supreme Court bench headed by Chief Justice Surya Kant. Investigators said eight people have been arrested so far, and that CCTV cameras captured around 70 suspicious incidents between April 27 and June 5, 2026, showing counting staff allegedly concealing wads of cash. The court has also issued notice to the temple trust seeking its response.
key_points: A special team files its sealed status report in the Supreme Court
8 people arrested; 70 suspicious cash-hiding incidents caught on CCTV
Counting staff allegedly pocketed donation money at the Ayodhya temple
Petitioners want a CBI probe and a forensic audit of the trust

depth.background: The Ram Mandir (Ram temple) in Ayodhya, Uttar Pradesh, is one of India's most prominent religious sites, run by a government-linked trust (the Shri Ram Janmabhoomi Teerth Kshetra Trust) that handles enormous public donations. Allegations surfaced that some of the cash pilgrims donated was being stolen during counting. Petitions were filed asking courts to order an independent investigation and audit of the temple's money.

stories.title: A flash flood tears through a Utah canyon and kills a firefighter's whole family — at least 8 dead as severe storms batter the US
stories.source: The New York Times (via Google News)
stories.what_happened: A family of five — a Provo, Utah fire captain, his wife and their three sons — was found dead after storms sent a flash flood surging through a canyon in southern Utah. Officials say at least eight people have died across the US as the same severe-weather system moved through. Local and national outlets (NYT, ABC News, Fox 13) reported the toll as search-and-rescue crews worked the flooded terrain.
key_points: A Provo fire captain, his wife and three sons killed
Sudden flood swept them away in a southern Utah canyon
At least 8 dead nationwide as storms hit the US
Flash floods give almost no warning in narrow canyons

depth.background: Flash floods are the deadliest kind of flooding because they arrive in minutes: heavy rain, sometimes from a storm miles away, funnels into a narrow canyon or dry streambed and turns it into a wall of fast water with little warning. The US has been under repeated severe-weather threats this summer, part of a pattern scientists link to a warmer atmosphere holding more moisture and dumping it in sudden, intense bursts.

sources.name: The New York Times (via Google News)

stories.title: Reliance beats forecasts with a record quarter — ₹23,001 crore profit on strong refining and Jio, and brokerages turn bullish
stories.what_happened: Reliance reported a June-quarter net profit of about ₹23,001 crore (roughly $2.7 billion), beating analysts' forecasts, alongside record quarterly core profit and EBITDA (a measure of operating earnings). Strong oil-refining margins and Jio's growth drove the beat, offsetting a softer retail performance. Brokerages responded warmly — JPMorgan headlined 'Finally, a beat', while Goldman Sachs stayed bullish and pointed to about 41% potential upside in the share price. The stock opened near ₹1,317 and traded up around 1% even as India's benchmark indexes fell.
key_points: Q1 net profit ₹23,001 crore — ahead of analysts' estimates
Driven by strong oil-refining (O2C) and telecom arm Jio; retail softer
JPMorgan: 'Finally, a beat'; Goldman Sachs sees ~41% upside
Shares rose ~1% even as the wider market fell

depth.background: Reliance Industries is India's most valuable listed company and a bellwether — a stock big enough that its results move the whole market's mood. It spans three main businesses: 'O2C' (oil-to-chemicals, i.e. refining crude oil and making petrochemicals), Jio (its telecom and digital arm), and retail. In recent quarters the company had underwhelmed investors, so this result was closely watched. India's April–June quarter is the first of its 2026-27 financial year (Q1FY27).

stories.title: Spain win the 2026 World Cup — a single extra-time goal ends Messi's last dance
stories.what_happened: In the final at MetLife Stadium in New Jersey on Sunday night, Spain beat Argentina 1-0. The game was goalless through 90 minutes and went to extra time, where Ferran Torres — a substitute who came on around the hour mark — scored the winner in the 106th minute. Spain smothered Argentina all night, keeping them to essentially no clear chances, while Argentina's goalkeeper Emiliano Martínez made a string of saves to keep the score down. Argentina's Enzo Fernández was sent off. It is Spain's second World Cup title. US President Donald Trump, who attended, handed over the trophy.
key_points: Spain beat Argentina 1-0 after extra time
Substitute Ferran Torres scored the only goal
Spain's second world title, first since 2010
Almost certainly Lionel Messi's final World Cup

depth.background: The FIFA World Cup is football's biggest event, held every four years, and the 2026 edition was co-hosted by the US, Canada and Mexico. Argentina were the defending champions (they won in 2022) and built their team around Lionel Messi, widely seen as the greatest player of his generation. Spain, a younger side that last won the trophy in 2010, came in as one of the tournament favourites after years of rebuilding.

thread.previously: We previewed the final at MetLife Stadium: Spain, powered by teenager Lamine Yamal, against Lionel Messi's Argentina, in what could be Messi's last World Cup match.
thread.changed: The match is over: Spain won 1-0 in extra time on Ferran Torres's goal, lifting their second World Cup and likely ending Messi's World Cup career.

prev_ref.title: The World Cup final is here: Spain vs Argentina, Messi vs Yamal — the planet's biggest match, tonight in New Jersey

stories.title: A mass shooting tears through downtown Tucson's nightlife strip — 9 wounded, and a police officer shoots the fleeing suspect
stories.what_happened: Around 2 a.m. on Sunday, July 19, a gunman opened fire on East Congress Street in downtown Tucson while the area was crowded with people out at night. Nine people were struck, most with wounds to their arms and legs, and were hospitalised; no deaths were reported. A responding Tucson police officer gave commands and then shot the suspect as he tried to flee, leaving him in life-threatening condition. Police said early indications point to an earlier confrontation between two groups.
key_points: Gunfire erupted ~2 a.m. on a packed Congress Street bar strip
Nine people shot, mostly in the arms and legs — no deaths reported
A police officer shot the fleeing suspect, left in critical condition
Police are probing whether a clash between two groups set it off

depth.background: Mass shootings — usually defined as an incident in which four or more people are shot — are a recurring feature of American life, and crowded nightlife districts are a frequent setting. Tucson, Arizona, has its own history with gun violence: in 2011 a gunman there shot Congresswoman Gabby Giffords and killed six people, an attack that helped launch a national gun-control movement. Congress Street is the city's main downtown entertainment strip, busy with bars and restaurants late into the weekend.

stories.title: New Zealand clinch the ODI series in the Caribbean with a nerve-shredding one-wicket win
stories.what_happened: In a low-scoring thriller on July 19, West Indies made 188, with left-arm spinner Gudakesh Motie's 5 for 47 keeping New Zealand's chase tight. New Zealand wobbled to 189 for 9 but got over the line by one wicket. Mark Chapman's 82-ball 80 was the backbone of the chase, and captain Mitchell Santner finished 34 not out, marshalling the tail — West Indies even dropped a chance with three runs needed before Santner sealed it. That gave New Zealand an unassailable 3-1 lead in the five-match series.
key_points: Black Caps beat West Indies by 1 wicket in Bridgetown, Barbados
Chasing 188, NZ stumbled to 189/9 — captain Mitchell Santner 34* saw them home
Mark Chapman's 80 held the innings together; Motie took a maiden ODI 5-for (5/47)
The win gives NZ an unbeatable 3-1 lead in the five-match series

depth.background: New Zealand's men's team, the Black Caps, are touring the West Indies for a five-match One Day International (ODI) series — the 50-overs-a-side format. Coming into the fourth match at Kensington Oval in Bridgetown, Barbados, the series was finely poised, and a win would put it beyond West Indies' reach.

stories.title: England beat India by 27 runs at Lord's to take the ODI series 2-1 — Duckett's record 141 trumps Rohit's 138
stories.what_happened: Batting first, England piled up 387 for 3 — the highest ODI total ever made at Lord's — powered by opener Ben Duckett's record-breaking 141. Chasing 388 to win, India came up 27 runs short, finishing on 360, despite a superb 138 from Rohit Sharma. England's bowlers 'refused to blink' to close out the 27-run win and take the series 2-1 — their first ODI series victory over India since 2018. Afterwards, a defiant Rohit dismissed speculation about retirement: 'Let the noise be there... No noise, no fun.'
key_points: England posted 387/3 — the highest-ever ODI total at Lord's
Ben Duckett hit a record 141; India fell 27 short despite Rohit's 138
England's first ODI series win over India since 2018
Rohit Sharma brushes off retirement talk: 'Let the noise be there'

depth.background: This was the decider of a three-match one-day international (ODI) series between England and India, level at 1-1: India had won the opener at Edgbaston, England hit back at Cardiff. It was played at Lord's in London — cricket's most famous ground. Extra spotlight fell on India captain Rohit Sharma, 38, whose place in the side and long-term future had been openly questioned going into the match.

thread.previously: Yesterday we reported England had piled up a mammoth 387/3 in the Lord's decider, leaving India a huge chase with Rohit Sharma's future in the spotlight.
thread.changed: The chase fell 27 runs short (India 360): England won the match and the series 2-1 — their first over India since 2018 — while Rohit made 138 and afterward brushed off retirement talk.

prev_ref.title: England pile up 387/3 in the Lord's ODI decider — India face a huge chase, and Rohit Sharma's future is on the line

stories.title: Verstappen's exit-clause deadline looms — and one race in Hungary could unlock F1's biggest transfer
stories.what_happened: Reports circulating around the Belgian Grand Prix weekend say Verstappen's exit clause can be activated if he is not in the top two of the drivers' standings by the end of this month — the point at which F1 breaks for its summer holiday after the Hungarian Grand Prix (July 24–26). With Antonelli winning at Spa and Red Bull's form patchy, that condition is now in live play. Analysts are also floating a domino scenario in which Antonelli moves to Ferrari, opening a Mercedes seat for Verstappen.
key_points: Max Verstappen has a contract clause that can free him early
It reportedly triggers if he's outside the top two by month-end
Antonelli's Spa win widened Mercedes' edge, fuelling the drama
A Verstappen-to-Mercedes move could set off a chain of transfers

depth.background: Formula 1's 'silly season' is the annual scramble over which drivers will race for which teams next year. This year it centres on four-time champion Max Verstappen, who is contracted to Red Bull through 2028 but is reportedly unhappy as key allies leave and his car struggles. Crucially, his deal is said to contain an exit clause — an escape hatch that lets him leave early if certain performance conditions aren't met. Mercedes, whose young star Kimi Antonelli is leading the 2026 title race, have long been seen as his most likely destination.

stories.title: At least 50 soldiers killed in Mali as fighters ambush an army convoy backed by Russian mercenaries
stories.what_happened: Fighters ambushed a convoy of Malian soldiers and Russia's Africa Corps mercenaries, killing at least 50, Al Jazeera reported. It is one of the deadlier single attacks on the joint force and shows how exposed even well-armed columns remain on the Sahel's vast, thinly-policed roads.
key_points: Ambush kills 50+ from a joint Malian army–Russia 'Africa Corps' convoy
The Sahel's jihadist insurgency keeps overrunning state forces
Russia's mercenaries are now taking heavy losses too

depth.background: Mali, in West Africa's Sahel (the dry belt just south of the Sahara), has battled a jihadist insurgency for over a decade. After coups in 2020–21, its military rulers expelled French and UN troops and hired Russian mercenaries — first the Wagner Group, now its state-run successor 'Africa Corps' — to fight the militants. The insurgency, linked to al-Qaeda and ISIS affiliates, has only spread across the region.

stories.title: India tells its citizens to leave Iran and postpone travel there as the US–Iran war escalates
stories.what_happened: India's Ministry of External Affairs (MEA) issued a travel advisory urging citizens to postpone non-essential travel to Iran and asking those already there to leave or exercise extreme caution, citing repeated US airstrikes and Iranian retaliation. It is a precautionary step — short of a full, organised evacuation.
key_points: MEA advisory: postpone all non-essential travel to Iran
Indians already in Iran urged to leave or stay extremely cautious
Follows days of US airstrikes and Iranian retaliation

depth.background: India has deep ties to the Gulf: millions of Indians live and work across the region, and India buys much of its oil from there. When Middle East conflicts flare, New Delhi's first job is protecting its nationals — it has run large evacuations before, such as from Ukraine in 2022 and Yemen in 2015. With the US–Iran fight now escalating sharply, Iran has become dangerous for foreigners.

stories.title: It wasn't just a crash: Mercedes says a battery glitch hit all its cars at Spa, wrecking Russell's race
stories.what_happened: After the race, Mercedes team boss Toto Wolff revealed the cause: all Mercedes-powered cars suffered a lack of 'energy deployment' — an unexplained battery/charging glitch — at the start. Russell was the biggest victim: from the Eau Rouge corner onward he lacked battery charge along the Kemmel straight, was overtaken, sank into the pack, and ultimately did not finish. Wolff said the team is still trying to understand the fault.
key_points: Toto Wolff: every Mercedes-powered car lost energy deployment at the start
Title contender George Russell 'the biggest victim' — dropped back, then out
The fault appeared from Eau Rouge along the Kemmel straight at Spa
A reliability worry as Mercedes fights for the 2026 title

depth.background: In Formula 1, a modern car's power unit combines a petrol engine with an electric-hybrid system that stores energy in a battery and 'deploys' it for extra speed — crucial on Spa-Francorchamps' long uphill straights. Mercedes has dominated 2026 qualifying, and its young driver Kimi Antonelli leads the championship. But at Sunday's Belgian Grand Prix, Antonelli's teammate and title rival George Russell — who started third — faded badly and failed to finish, a result first reported simply as him dropping out early.

thread.previously: Yesterday we reported Antonelli won the Belgian GP while his title rival George Russell dropped out early — framed as him crashing/failing to finish on the opening lap.
thread.changed: Now the cause is out: Mercedes says a battery 'energy-deployment' glitch hit all its cars at the start, with Russell the biggest victim — turning it from a racing incident into an unresolved reliability problem.

prev_ref.title: Antonelli wins the Belgian Grand Prix — holding off Leclerc as title rival Russell crashes out on lap one

stories.title: The US and Iran are now trading direct blows — two US soldiers killed in Jordan, and America hits back with airstrikes
stories.what_happened: Iran attacked a base in Jordan that hosts US forces, killing two American service members and leaving one missing, the US military said. In response, the US launched new airstrikes it said would 'swiftly punish' Iran. Iran says US strikes hit an under-construction nuclear power plant; it also struck a Kuwaiti electricity-and-water desalination plant — the second such hit in two days — and warned it was firing toward Jordan, with warnings the violence could spill into Israel. The reporting is fast-moving and still developing.
key_points: Two US soldiers killed, one missing, after Iran struck a base in Jordan
US launched fresh airstrikes to 'swiftly punish' Iran
Iran hit a Kuwaiti water-and-power plant and fired toward Jordan
Both sides edging toward all-out war near the Strait of Hormuz

depth.background: The US and Iran have been enemies for decades, but they mostly fought through proxies (allied armed groups), sanctions and cyberattacks rather than head-on. Over the past roughly eight days that restraint broke down: a crisis around the Strait of Hormuz — the narrow sea gate through which about a fifth of the world's oil is shipped — spiralled into an exchange of strikes across the Middle East. Until now the fighting mostly hit Iran's allies and infrastructure; the killing of US troops pulls Washington in directly.

thread.previously: Yesterday we covered Iran striking Kuwait's water-and-power desalination plants, spreading the Gulf war to civilian lifelines and forcing rationing.
thread.changed: Now the US is directly in the fight: Iran killed two US soldiers at a base in Jordan, and Washington has launched retaliatory airstrikes to 'punish' Iran — pushing both sides toward open war.

prev_ref.title: Iran hits Kuwait's water-and-power plants, forcing rationing — the Gulf war spreads to civilian lifelines

stories.title: 20 Trinamool MPs break from Mamata Banerjee to merge with a tiny party — a legal workaround to defect without losing their seats
stories.what_happened: A rebel group of 20 TMC Lok Sabha MPs — led by figures including Kakoli Ghosh Dastidar and Sudip Bandyopadhyay — informed Lok Sabha Speaker Om Birla they are merging with the Tripura-based Nationalist Citizens Party of India (NCPI) and asked to be recognised as a separate bloc. Because 20 of TMC's 28 MPs (more than two-thirds) are crossing over, the move is designed to satisfy the anti-defection rule. The government arranged for the group to attend the pre-session all-party meeting, prompting the wider opposition to stage a symbolic walkout.
key_points: 20 of TMC's 28 Lok Sabha MPs are quitting Mamata's leadership
They plan to merge into the little-known Nationalist Citizens Party (NCPI)
The merger route dodges India's anti-defection law
Opposition walked out of an all-party meet in protest over recognising them

depth.background: The Trinamool Congress (TMC), led by Mamata Banerjee, has run West Bengal for over a decade but suffered a heavy defeat in the state's 2026 Assembly elections. In India, lawmakers who switch parties normally lose their seats under the 'anti-defection law' (the Constitution's Tenth Schedule) — but there is a loophole: if two-thirds or more of a party's legislators merge as a bloc into another party, it counts as a legal merger, not defection.

stories.title: China's Moonshot AI moves to cash in on its 'Kimi' moment — planning a Hong Kong listing within six months at a $30bn-plus valuation
stories.what_happened: According to Bloomberg, Moonshot has formally sent its shareholders a resolution seeking their approval for a stock-market listing (IPO) in Hong Kong, telling backers it could go public in as little as six months. At the same time it is closing a fundraising round that would value the company at more than $30 billion. An IPO — 'initial public offering' — is the first time a private company sells its shares to the public, turning private paper wealth into real, tradable cash.
key_points: Moonshot tells investors it wants to IPO in as little as six months
It is raising money at a valuation above $30 billion
The push follows its Kimi K3 model rattling global tech stocks
A Hong Kong listing would be one of China's biggest AI debuts

depth.background: Moonshot AI is a three-year-old Beijing startup and one of China's leading builders of large AI 'models' — the software behind chatbots and coding assistants. On July 17 it released Kimi K3, a huge 'open-weight' model (meaning the finished model is free for anyone to download and run), which it says outperformed America's best on coding tests. That launch spooked investors, who fear cheap Chinese models could undercut the pricey US AI industry, and it triggered a sharp sell-off in chip and tech shares — a repeat of the 'DeepSeek moment' from early 2025.

thread.previously: On July 17 Moonshot unveiled Kimi K3, a roughly 2.8-trillion-parameter open model it billed as the world's largest, and its arrival sparked a chip-stock sell-off across Asia and the US on fears cheap Chinese AI would undercut American rivals.
thread.changed: Now Moonshot is moving to cash in on the buzz — telling investors it plans a Hong Kong IPO within six months and closing a funding round that values it at more than $30 billion.

prev_ref.title: China's Moonshot AI unveils Kimi K3 — a giant, cheap open model that rivals America's best and triggers a 'DeepSeek moment' chip selloff

stories.title: A second person dies in a Legionnaires' disease outbreak on Manhattan's Upper East Side — 72 sickened, but officials say the source is likely gone
stories.what_happened: The New York City Health Department said a second person has died in a cluster of Legionnaires' cases centred on the Upper East Side of Manhattan. In all, 72 people have been infected: two have died, nine are currently in hospital, 50 have already been discharged and 11 did not need hospital care. Officials have been testing the cooling towers of nearby buildings and ordering owners to drain and disinfect any that test positive.
key_points: 72 people infected on Manhattan's Upper East Side, 2 now dead
9 people are still hospitalised; most others have recovered
Cause: Legionella bacteria that breeds in warm, stagnant water
Officials suspect building cooling towers and say cases are falling

depth.background: Legionnaires' disease is a severe form of pneumonia (a lung infection) caused by breathing in tiny water droplets carrying Legionella bacteria, which thrive in warm, stagnant water. It is not spread person-to-person — outbreaks are usually traced to man-made water systems, most often the 'cooling towers' that sit on top of large buildings and shed a fine mist as part of their air-conditioning. New York City, with thousands of such towers, sees clusters most summers when the weather is hot.

stories.title: A wildfire north of Madrid burns 13,000 hectares and empties 16 villages as Spain braces for another heatwave
stories.what_happened: A major wildfire that broke out on Thursday near the village of La Mierla in Guadalajara province — roughly 100 km north of Madrid — has burned about 13,000 hectares and forced the evacuation of 16 villages, affecting more than 700 residents. It is one of several large blazes: another around 15,400 hectares has burned near Orés in the northeast, pushing Spain's total burned area this year to about 70,000 hectares. Forecasters warn a third heatwave, with temperatures above 40°C, begins Tuesday.
key_points: The La Mierla fire has scorched about 13,000 hectares
16 villages evacuated; more than 700 people affected
Spain has already burned roughly 70,000 hectares in 2026
A third heatwave, with 40°C+ temperatures, starts Tuesday

depth.background: Spain, like much of southern Europe, faces longer, hotter and drier summers as the climate warms, turning forests into tinder. Wildfires have become a near-annual crisis, and 2026 has been especially punishing. A 'hectare' is a unit of area equal to about one-and-a-half football pitches — so 13,000 hectares is a vast stretch of countryside.

stories.title: Madhya Pradesh clears a Uniform Civil Code draft that makes registering a live-in relationship compulsory — skip it, and you could face jail
stories.what_happened: On Sunday, July 19, the Madhya Pradesh cabinet approved a draft UCC bill. It criminalises triple talaq and 'nikah halala', prohibits polygamy, and makes couples register a live-in relationship within a month — with up to three months in jail or a ₹10,000 fine for failing to do so, and stiffer penalties for giving false information or ignoring a registrar's notice. It also gives children born from live-in relationships legal status and equal inheritance rights, lets an abandoned female partner claim maintenance, and exempts Scheduled Tribes. The bill will be introduced when the state assembly's session begins Monday.
key_points: MP cabinet approved the draft UCC bill on Sunday
Live-in of over a month without registering: up to 3 months' jail or ₹10,000 fine
Bans polygamy, triple talaq and 'nikah halala'; equal inheritance for all children
Scheduled Tribes are exempt; bill goes to the assembly Monday

depth.background: A Uniform Civil Code (UCC) is a single set of laws on marriage, divorce, inheritance and adoption that applies to everyone regardless of religion — replacing the separate 'personal laws' Hindus, Muslims, Christians and others currently follow. India's Constitution (Article 44) lists a UCC as a goal for the state to 'endeavour' towards, and the ruling BJP has long championed it; Uttarakhand became the first state to enact one. Madhya Pradesh, also BJP-run, is now moving to follow.

stories.title: After his Spa win, Antonelli's title lead balloons to 45 points — and Ferrari is fined for a pit-lane scare
stories.what_happened: With the Spa result confirmed, Antonelli extended his lead to 45 points over Hamilton, who reclaimed second place, while Russell dropped to 50 points behind after his DNF ('did not finish'). Separately, stewards fined Ferrari €30,000 (€10,000 suspended) for an 'unsafe release': during Hamilton's pit stop, a mechanic reaching in for a front-wing adjustment was struck by the car as it was waved off. The mechanic was unhurt, and because Hamilton stopped immediately and lost time, stewards issued a fine rather than a track penalty, so he keeps fourth.
key_points: Antonelli's championship lead over Hamilton grows to 45 points
Russell, out on lap one, is now 50 points adrift and fading
Ferrari fined €30,000 after Hamilton's car hit a mechanic in the pits
Stewards spared Hamilton a sporting penalty; he keeps 4th

depth.background: Kimi Antonelli, Mercedes' teenage star, has led the 2026 Formula 1 championship for much of the season, with team-mate George Russell his closest rival. At the Belgian Grand Prix at Spa-Francorchamps, Antonelli started on pole and won — his sixth victory of the year — while Russell retired on the opening lap after a collision with Ferrari's Lewis Hamilton. In F1, drivers score points by finishing position, and the season title goes to whoever accumulates the most across all races.

stories.title: India and the EU say their long-delayed free-trade deal is nearly done — aiming to sign by year-end and switch it on in early 2027
stories.what_happened: Commerce and Industry Minister Piyush Goyal concluded a July 13–18 visit to Spain, Belgium, Finland and Estonia, meeting EU leaders including European Council President Antonio Costa and Trade Commissioner Maros Sefcovic. Both sides reaffirmed their commitment to sign and implement the FTA early, and Goyal said the plan is to finalise the legal text before the end of 2026 and bring it into effect in the first quarter of 2027 (around February–March). Talks also covered an investment-protection agreement, market access for farm goods, Basmati rice, steel scrap, and the EU's carbon border tax.
key_points: Trade Minister Piyush Goyal wraps a five-day, four-nation Europe tour
Both sides reaffirm early signing and implementation of the FTA
Target: sign the legal text by end-2026, in force February–March 2027
Sticking points: farm goods, Basmati rice, steel scrap, EU's carbon border tax

depth.background: A free-trade agreement (FTA) is a deal to cut or scrap the taxes (tariffs) two sides charge on each other's goods, making trade cheaper and faster. India and the European Union — a 27-country bloc that is one of India's biggest trading partners — first started FTA talks back in 2007, then stalled for years over disagreements, and relaunched negotiations in 2022. After India recently sealed a trade pact with the UK, momentum has picked up.

stories.title: Bangladesh come from behind to clinch the T20 series in Zimbabwe — Tanzid Hasan's unbeaten 66 seals a nervy chase
stories.what_happened: Chasing Zimbabwe's 143 for 7, Bangladesh reached 144 for 6 with two balls to spare to win by four wickets and take the series 2-1 — their first T20I series win of 2026. Opener Tanzid Hasan held the innings together with an unbeaten 66 off 58 balls, his 13th T20I fifty. The finish was dramatic: an umpire's 'dead ball' call (ruling a delivery void because a fielder was moving illegally) denied Zimbabwe's Brad Evans a hat-trick, and Mahedi Hasan struck the winning boundary.
key_points: Bangladesh won the decider by 4 wickets to take the series 2-1
They fought back after losing the opening match
Tanzid Hasan anchored the chase with an unbeaten 66
A controversial 'dead ball' denied Zimbabwe's Evans a hat-trick

depth.background: Bangladesh and Zimbabwe were playing a three-match Twenty20 International (T20I) series in Bulawayo, Zimbabwe. T20 is cricket's shortest, fastest format — 20 overs a side — and a three-match series is decided by whoever wins two. Bangladesh had lost the first game, meaning the third and final match was a winner-takes-all decider.

stories.title: Antonelli wins the Belgian Grand Prix — holding off Leclerc as title rival Russell crashes out on lap one
stories.what_happened: Antonelli turned pole into victory, winning the 2026 Belgian Grand Prix for the first time — his sixth career win. He survived an opening-lap attack and an awkward Virtual Safety Car period, lost the lead briefly when Ferrari pitted Charles Leclerc at the right moment, then re-passed Leclerc on the Kemmel Straight on lap 34 to win. His championship rival and team-mate George Russell retired on the very first lap after contact with Lewis Hamilton, who was handed a 5-second penalty. Max Verstappen, the four-time champion, came home third.
key_points: Kimi Antonelli converts pole into his 6th career win at Spa
Passes Charles Leclerc on the Kemmel Straight on lap 34 to retake the lead
Title rival George Russell out on lap 1 after contact with Hamilton
Hamilton given a 5-second penalty; Verstappen finishes 3rd

depth.background: Formula 1 is the world's top motor-racing series: 20 drivers, 10 teams, points at every round, and a season-long fight for the drivers' championship. Kimi Antonelli, the 19-year-old Italian in a Mercedes, has emerged this year as the sport's breakout star and is locked in a title fight — including with his own Mercedes team-mate George Russell. Yesterday Antonelli took pole (the fastest qualifying lap, which earns the No. 1 starting spot) at Spa-Francorchamps, the long, fast, weather-prone circuit in the Belgian hills that drivers rate as the best on the calendar.

stories.title: Red Bull picks Tom Hart as Max Verstappen's next race engineer as long-time voice Lambiase heads for McLaren
stories.what_happened: Red Bull has identified Tom Hart as Verstappen's next race engineer, a name Verstappen already favoured within the team. Hart had been Verstappen's performance engineer before taking a wider role covering both Verstappen and rookie team-mate Isack Hadjar. He had been lined up to become Alex Albon's race engineer at Williams, but Red Bull persuaded Williams to reverse that transfer and keep him. Hart is expected to step up at the start of 2027, while Lambiase will move on — reportedly joining McLaren as chief racing officer in 2028, likely after a period of 'gardening leave'.
key_points: Red Bull names Tom Hart to become Verstappen's race engineer, likely from 2027
He replaces Gianpiero 'GP' Lambiase, Verstappen's engineer since 2016
Lambiase is set to join McLaren in 2028 as chief racing officer
Red Bull blocked Hart's planned move to Williams to keep him

depth.background: In Formula 1, the 'race engineer' is the single voice a driver hears on the radio during a race — the person who manages strategy, tyres, car settings and calm under pressure. For a decade, that voice for four-time champion Max Verstappen has been Gianpiero Lambiase ('GP'), one of the sport's most famous driver-engineer partnerships. When such a long-standing pairing changes, it's genuinely significant behind the scenes.

stories.title: Exam-leak anger reaches Parliament: opposition will demand Education Minister Dharmendra Pradhan quit as the monsoon session opens tomorrow
stories.what_happened: Ahead of the monsoon session opening July 20, opposition parties signalled they will demand the resignation of Union Education Minister Dharmendra Pradhan over the exam-leak scandal. Congress's B.K. Hariprasad, echoing Rahul Gandhi's 'Chhatron ki Goonj' campaign, alleged 152 paper leaks occurred between 2014 and 2026. Notably, Odisha's Naveen Patnaik (BJD) — often seen as non-aligned — urged the government to talk to protesting students, warning that 'a nation that compromises the integrity of its examination system compromises its future.' The government will lay out its legislative agenda at an all-party meeting. The session runs to August 13.
key_points: Opposition to target Education Minister over exam paper leaks
Congress cites '152 paper leaks' between 2014 and 2026
Even BJD's Naveen Patnaik urges talks with protesting students
Monsoon session runs July 20 to August 13

depth.background: For weeks, a youth-led movement has been building over repeated leaks of exam question papers — including for high-stakes tests like NEET, the national medical-entrance exam that decides who becomes a doctor. Leaks let some cheat while honest students lose seats, and the anger has spilled into street protests (the 'cockroach' march) and demands for accountability. That anger now moves from the streets into Parliament as the monsoon session begins.

stories.title: Iran hits Kuwait's water-and-power plants, forcing rationing — the Gulf war spreads to civilian lifelines
stories.what_happened: Early Saturday, July 18, a fire broke out at a second Kuwaiti power-generation and water-desalination plant after an Iranian strike — the second such facility hit within 24 hours. Kuwait's energy ministry took several generating units offline 'to preserve the safety of the facility and the stability of the grid' and urged residents to ration power at peak hours. Iran's Revolutionary Guard (IRGC) said it targeted US troop-deployment sites, launch pads and HIMARS rocket systems in Kuwait; Kuwait said the plants themselves were damaged. No casualties were reported, and by July 19 one plant was still burning.
key_points: Iran struck a second Kuwaiti power-and-desalination plant in 24 hours
Fire forced generating units offline; Kuwait told people to ration electricity
About 90% of Kuwait's drinking water comes from desalination
War with the US now hits Gulf civilians' basic services, not just bases

depth.background: The US and Iran have been in an escalating shooting war for over a week — the US bombing near Tehran, Iranian missiles killing US troops in Jordan. Kuwait is a small, oil-rich US ally on the Persian Gulf that hosts American forces, so Iran has been firing at US bases there and now at infrastructure. Crucially, Kuwait has almost no natural fresh water: it turns seawater into drinking water at big coastal desalination plants that also generate much of its electricity.

thread.previously: We reported the US had widened its air war — bombing near Tehran for the first time and disabling an Iranian tanker — as Iran fired back at US bases across the Gulf.
thread.changed: Iran's retaliation has now moved from US bases to civilian lifelines: it has hit two Kuwaiti water-desalination and power plants within 24 hours, forcing Kuwait to ration electricity.

prev_ref.title: US widens its war on Iran — bombing near Tehran for the first time and disabling a tanker, as Iran fires back at American bases in the Gulf

stories.title: Tate brothers arrested in Miami as UK seeks their extradition on 59 charges including rape and trafficking
stories.what_happened: US Marshals arrested the brothers on Saturday in Miami on a sealed warrant after British prosecutors announced a total of 59 charges and requested their extradition. Andrew Tate faces 42 charges including rape, human trafficking, indecent images of a child and assault; Tristan faces 17 including rape, sexual assault and trafficking. The UK's Crown Prosecution Service says the offences involve seven women in the East of England between 2010 and 2017. Andrew's lawyers say the claims are false and any activity was consensual.
key_points: US Marshals arrest Andrew and Tristan Tate in Miami on a sealed warrant
UK announces 59 charges tied to 7 alleged victims (2010–2017)
Andrew faces 42 charges, Tristan 17; both deny wrongdoing
Britain is now seeking their extradition from the US

depth.background: Andrew Tate is a former kickboxer turned online personality with a huge, mostly young-male following, known for 'hyper-masculine' content that critics call misogynistic. He and his brother Tristan have faced criminal investigations in several countries for years, including a separate case in Romania. This is the first time UK charges have led to their arrest on US soil.

stories.title: India's Gen Z 'cockroach' movement calls a march on Parliament — a youth revolt over exam leaks builds as the monsoon session opens
stories.what_happened: On Sunday, July 19, 2026, organisers confirmed a 'Chalo Sansad' ('Let's go to Parliament') march from Jantar Mantar to Parliament on Monday, July 20 — the first day of Parliament's monsoon session. The Cockroach Janta Party issued do's and don'ts (carry the national flag, no political-party flags, stay peaceful), and Wangchuk called it India's 'second freedom movement.' Their core demands: fix the exam system, act hard on paper leaks, and remove Union Education Minister Dharmendra Pradhan. Delhi Police deployed extra forces and said an unauthorised march toward Parliament would not be allowed.
key_points: 'Chalo Sansad' (Let's go to Parliament) march set for July 20
Led by the youth 'Cockroach Janta Party' over exam-paper leaks
Demands: exam reform and the education minister's resignation
Follows activist Sonam Wangchuk's forced hospitalisation

depth.background: For years, India's students have been rocked by exam-paper 'leaks' — where the questions for high-stakes national tests get stolen and sold in advance, wrecking the results for millions who studied honestly. That anger fused with a broader Gen Z frustration into a loose youth movement that ironically calls itself the 'Cockroach Janta Party' — reclaiming an insult about ordinary people being crushed and ignored. Its most prominent backer, engineer-activist Sonam Wangchuk, has been on a hunger strike at Delhi's Jantar Mantar protest ground and was forcibly moved to a government hospital on Saturday.

thread.previously: Yesterday Delhi Police forcibly moved a fasting Sonam Wangchuk to a government hospital on day 21 of his hunger strike, and a court declined to order his release.
thread.changed: Today the youth movement behind him turned the anger into a mass 'Chalo Sansad' march on Parliament for July 20, with Wangchuk branding it a 'second freedom movement' and police vowing to block any unauthorised march.

prev_ref.title: Police forcibly move a fasting Sonam Wangchuk to hospital on day 21 of his Ladakh protest

stories.title: Hundreds of Israelis march on the Gaza border to demand Jewish resettlement — with coalition ministers backing them
stories.what_happened: Hundreds of activists marched from the town of Sderot toward the Gaza border on Sunday, July 19, demanding that Israel re-establish settlements inside Gaza. The military issued a 'closed military zone' order over parts of the western Negev and physically blocked marchers at roadblocks; organisers said a Likud minister joined the group as it pushed past an IDF checkpoint, and other senior coalition figures were expected to attend. The army and organisers negotiated so the crowd did not actually enter Gaza.
key_points: Marchers chanted 'Ours forever', demanding settlements be rebuilt in Gaza
The IDF declared the border a closed military zone and blocked them
Senior government ministers backed or joined the pro-settlement rally
It clashes with Israel's official stance that it won't resettle Gaza

depth.background: Israel withdrew all its settlers and soldiers from the Gaza Strip in 2005. A hard-right movement in Israel has long wanted to reverse that and rebuild Jewish settlements there — a goal that gained momentum during the long Gaza war. The march was organised by settler groups such as Nahala, and several ministers in Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu's governing coalition openly support the idea.

stories.title: England pile up 387/3 in the Lord's ODI decider — India face a huge chase, and Rohit Sharma's future is on the line
stories.what_happened: England won the toss, chose to bat, and piled up a mammoth 387 for 3. Opener Ben Duckett smashed 141, and Joe Root became the first England batter to register six consecutive ODI half-centuries, a record run of form. India's bowlers had a chastening day, and India began their reply needing to chase down a target near 388 — one of the tougher chases in one-day cricket.
key_points: Series level 1-1; this third ODI at Lord's decides it
England batted first and posted a mammoth 387/3
Ben Duckett hit 141; Joe Root made a record 6th straight ODI fifty
India's chase — and captain Rohit Sharma's place — under the spotlight

depth.background: India and England are playing a three-match One-Day International (ODI) series — the 50-overs-a-side format. India won the first game, England the second, so the sides went to the final match at Lord's in London — cricket's most famous ground — tied 1-1, making it a winner-takes-all decider. Around it swirls a bigger question: the future of India's veteran captain Rohit Sharma, whose place in the one-day side has been debated as selectors look ahead.

thread.previously: England levelled the series 1-1 in Cardiff (guided by Joe Root's unbeaten 99), setting up a winner-takes-all third ODI at Lord's with Rohit Sharma's future in focus.
thread.changed: The decider is now underway: England batted first and posted a huge 387/3 (Duckett 141, Root a record 6th straight fifty), leaving India a daunting chase to win the series.

prev_ref.title: India vs England ODI series goes to a Lord's decider — with Rohit Sharma's future in the spotlight

stories.title: The talent-spotter who found Antonelli is leaving Mercedes for Red Bull
stories.what_happened: Multiple outlets report that Lagrue is set to leave Mercedes to take a similar talent-development role at Red Bull. It's an unusually visible transfer for a figure who normally works out of the spotlight, and it comes mid-season with Antonelli leading the championship for Mercedes.
key_points: Gwen Lagrue, Mercedes' junior-driver boss, set to join Red Bull
He ran Mercedes' young-driver programme since 2016
He is credited with discovering star rookie Kimi Antonelli
A rare high-profile move among F1's behind-the-scenes talent scouts

depth.background: In Formula 1, most attention goes to the drivers, but teams also compete fiercely for the people who find and develop future stars. Gwen Lagrue has led Mercedes' junior driver programme since 2016 and is widely credited with spotting Kimi Antonelli, the young Italian now fighting for the 2026 drivers' title.

stories.title: PV Sindhu wins the Japan Open — a first for any Indian, and her biggest title since 2019
stories.what_happened: On Sunday, July 19, 2026, Sindhu beat Japan's Akane Yamaguchi 21-17, 21-17 in the women's singles final in Tokyo to win the BWF Japan Open — becoming the first Indian to lift the title. The straight-games win over a home favourite ended a drought of more than two years and stands as her most significant title since the 2019 World Championships.
key_points: Beat home favourite Akane Yamaguchi 21-17, 21-17
First Indian ever to win the Japan Open singles title
Ends a title drought of more than two years
Her biggest crown since the 2019 World Championship

depth.background: PV Sindhu, 31, is India's most decorated badminton player — a two-time Olympic medallist and 2019 world champion — but she had gone through a long lean patch, without a top-tier tour title since the Syed Modi International in 2024.
