An eight-month ISS mission, returning to Earth around April 2027
His father hails from Ottapalam in Kerala's Palakkad district

depth.background: Anil Menon, born and raised in Minneapolis, is of Indo-Ukrainian descent — his father is from Ottapalam in Kerala's Palakkad district, his mother from Ukraine. A physician and mechanical engineer, he served as a US Space Force flight surgeon and pilot, helped SpaceX build its medical team, and was selected as a NASA astronaut in 2021. This is his first spaceflight.

thread.previously: Yesterday we flagged Menon's launch as a one-line notable — an Indian-origin NASA astronaut heading to the ISS aboard a Russian Soyuz, a rare feel-good India-in-the-world moment.
thread.changed: Today we decode who he is — his Kerala–Ukraine roots and career as a doctor and Space Force flight surgeon — and the full eight-month mission, now confirmed underway.

stories.title: A chemical tanker burns off Oman: the Stolt Magnesium is hit by an external blast near Hormuz as Gulf shipping comes under sustained attack
stories.source: Reuters (Chemical tanker Stolt Magnesium hit by explosion off Omani coast, manager says)
stories.what_happened: The Stolt Magnesium caught fire after what its manager described as the 'explosion of an unidentified external device' while sailing in the Arabian Sea off Oman's coast, roughly 40 nautical miles northeast of Qalhat. The blast, at about 12:40 a.m. local time, started a fire in the engine room; all seafarers were reported safe and accounted for and began fighting the blaze. The UK Maritime Trade Operations (UKMTO) - the Royal Navy body that tracks regional shipping - logged an incident at that location. It follows other strikes in the same waters: UAE-flagged supertankers Mombasa B and Al Bahyah were hit by Iranian cruise missiles transiting Hormuz, damaging the ships and killing one crew member.
key_points: Norwegian-managed chemical tanker Stolt Magnesium hit by an external device off Oman
Blast sparked an engine-room fire ~40 nautical miles NE of Qalhat; all crew safe
One of a string of Gulf shipping attacks during the US-Iran Hormuz war
Keeps Brent near $85-86, a one-month high, on the shipping-risk premium

depth.background: The Strait of Hormuz is a narrow sea lane between Iran and Oman through which about a fifth of the world's seaborne oil must pass, with no easy detour. This year's US-Iran war has turned the surrounding waters into a shooting gallery: over the past week tankers have been struck, crew killed, and traffic has thinned as owners avoid the risk. The Stolt Magnesium is a chemical tanker (a ship built to carry liquid chemicals rather than crude oil) managed by Norway's Stolt-Nielsen.

sources.name: Reuters (Chemical tanker Stolt Magnesium hit by explosion off Omani coast, manager says)
South China Morning Post (Norwegian chemical tanker hit by projectile off Oman amid US-Iran tensions)
TradeWinds (Stolt chemical tanker struck by 'projectile' off Oman)

thread.previously: Yesterday we reported Iran claiming it hit two oil supertankers in the Gulf, and India summoning Iran's deputy envoy after an Indian sailor was killed aboard one of the tankers, as the US naval blockade of Iran's ports went live.
thread.changed: The attacks spread to chemical shipping: a Norwegian-managed chemical tanker, the Stolt Magnesium, was hit by an external blast off Oman near Hormuz and caught fire (crew safe), keeping Brent pinned near a one-month high.

stories.title: India bans imports of goods made with forced labour — a first, and a move aimed at heading off US tariffs
stories.source: The Hindu (India bans the import of goods made using forced labour, even as U.S. investigation is pending)
stories.what_happened: India amended its Foreign Trade Policy to formally prohibit the import of goods made using forced labour, aligning its rules with ILO standards for the first time. Under the change, the DGFT can launch an inquiry and then order a ban on specific products or sources found to involve forced labour, giving the government a concrete legal tool it previously lacked. The step lands while Washington is in the process of releasing its final decision on tariffs it plans to impose on countries it says have not done enough to stop the trade in forced-labour goods — making India's timing look deliberate.
key_points: India amends its trade policy to prohibit imports of goods produced using forced labour
Aligns the country with international labour (ILO) standards for the first time
The DGFT can now order specific bans after an inquiry finds forced labour
Timing is pointed: the US is about to decide on tariffs over forced-labour goods

depth.background: 'Forced labour' means work people are made to do against their will — through coercion, debt bondage, trafficking or threats — and it taints supply chains for goods like cotton, electronics and minerals worldwide. The International Labour Organization (ILO), a UN body, sets the global standards against it. India regulates what can be imported through its Foreign Trade Policy, enforced by the DGFT (Directorate General of Foreign Trade, the arm of the commerce ministry that writes import-export rules). Until now India had no explicit power to block goods simply because they were made with forced labour. This move arrives as the United States finalises a decision on tariffs against trading partners it judges to have done too little to keep such goods out.

sources.name: The Hindu (India bans the import of goods made using forced labour, even as U.S. investigation is pending)
Livemint (Explained: Why India has banned imports of goods made with forced labour)
Reuters (India to ban import of goods made using forced labour amid US probe)

stories.title: Ukraine opens a new front at sea: drone strikes choke Russian shipping in the Sea of Azov
stories.source: Al Jazeera (Russia readies to reroute exports from Sea of Azov after Ukrainian attacks)
stories.what_happened: Ukraine's Unmanned Systems Forces say they have struck scores of Russian 'shadow fleet' and cargo vessels in the Sea of Azov - reports cite 15 hit in one overnight raid and roughly 100-116 vessels over about nine days, including tankers, dry-cargo ships and tugs. Russia reportedly halted shipping through the Don-Azov canal and stopped traffic through the Kerch Strait after the attacks, and is preparing to reroute exports. Kyiv frames the campaign as part of a wider effort to isolate Crimea and hit the energy and export revenue that funds Russia's war. Russia has confirmed some strikes, but the full tally is Ukraine's claim and hard to verify independently.
key_points: Ukraine says it has struck 100+ Russian vessels in the Sea of Azov in about nine days
Russia reportedly halted shipping through the Don-Azov canal and Kerch Strait
The Azov handles ~25% of Russia's wheat exports - a global grain-price risk
Part of a campaign to isolate Crimea and squeeze Russia's war economy

depth.background: The Sea of Azov is a small, shallow sea in Russia's south, connected to the Black Sea by the narrow Kerch Strait beside occupied Crimea. Russia has used it as a sheltered route for oil, grain and military cargo, largely out of reach of Ukraine's earlier weapons. Over the war Ukraine has increasingly relied on cheap naval and aerial drones to strike far behind the front line. Russia is the world's largest wheat exporter, and a big share of that grain sails from Azov ports.

sources.name: Al Jazeera (Russia readies to reroute exports from Sea of Azov after Ukrainian attacks)
France 24 (Kyiv claims drone attacks on 11 Russian tankers in Sea of Azov)
The Kyiv Independent (Russia halts shipping through Don-Azov canal after Ukrainian strike on vessels, Reuters reports)

stories.title: India's direct tax collections up 16% to Rs 6.51 lakh crore early in FY27
stories.what_happened: The Income Tax Department reported that net direct tax collection (what the government keeps after paying out refunds) rose 16.4% from a year earlier to over Rs 6.51 lakh crore as of July 13, early in FY27. The standout was corporate tax, up about 22% to Rs 2.40 lakh crore, with non-corporate tax — mostly individuals — also growing healthily. On a gross basis, before refunds, collections were up around 16% to roughly Rs 7.73 lakh crore. The government has budgeted Rs 26.97 lakh crore of direct taxes for the full year, nearly 15% more than FY26.
key_points: Net direct tax collection up 16.4% year-on-year so far in FY27
Corporate tax the engine — up 22% to Rs 2.40 lakh crore
Gross collections (before refunds) up ~16% to Rs 7.73 lakh crore
Full-year target is Rs 26.97 lakh crore, ~15% above last year

depth.background: The government funds itself mainly through taxes, split into two buckets. 'Direct taxes' are levied straight on income and profits — corporate tax paid by companies and income tax paid by individuals — and you cannot pass them on to someone else. 'Indirect taxes' like GST sit on spending. Direct-tax numbers are watched closely because they move with how much companies are earning and how much people are being paid, making them a real-time read on the economy's health. India's financial year 'FY27' runs from April 2026 to March 2027.

stories.title: China expels Politburo member Ma Xingrui in a widening purge
stories.what_happened: The Communist Party expelled Ma Xingrui and referred him for prosecution after an investigation opened in April. Authorities accuse him of taking bribes, rigging official appointments, illegally accepting gifts, helping relatives buy property below market price, and 'trading power and money for sex.' He is the third member of the current Politburo to fall since 2025 — following the expulsion of top general He Weidong and an investigation into Zhang Youxia, the military's most senior officer — a striking concentration of purges at the very top of the system.
key_points: Ma Xingrui expelled from the Communist Party over graft and 'sex' charges
Third member of the ~24-strong Politburo brought down since 2025
A rocket scientist who rose to run restive Xinjiang
Signal that Xi's anti-corruption drive still reaches the very top

depth.background: China is a one-party state run by the Communist Party, and at its apex sits the Politburo — roughly two dozen of the country's most powerful officials who help set national policy. Leader Xi Jinping has waged a sweeping 'anti-corruption' campaign since 2012 that has removed hundreds of thousands of officials; supporters call it a clean-up, critics say it doubles as a tool to purge rivals and enforce loyalty. Ma Xingrui was a rocket-scientist-turned-politician: he ran China's main state aerospace and missile maker, later governed the wealthy province of Guangdong, and most recently served as Party chief of Xinjiang, the tightly-controlled far-western region.

stories.title: First clinical hint that Ozempic/Wegovy may slow biological aging
stories.what_happened: A team at UC San Diego ran a randomized, double-blind, placebo-controlled trial — the gold-standard design — in 108 adults with HIV-associated fat changes, giving about half weekly semaglutide and half a dummy shot for 32 weeks. On one epigenetic clock (called DunedinPACE), the pace of biological aging slowed by roughly 9% in the treated group; a second measure linked to age-related disease and death risk also improved, and telomere-length markers rose in nearly half of participants. The researchers, publishing in a peer-reviewed journal, called it the first clinical evidence that the drug may influence human aging.
key_points: Small trial: ~9% slower aging on an epigenetic 'clock'
108 adults with HIV-related fat changes, 32 weeks vs placebo
'Biological age' isn't your birthday age — it's cellular wear
Early, narrow evidence — not proof the drug extends life

depth.background: Semaglutide — sold as Ozempic for diabetes and Wegovy for weight loss — belongs to a class of drugs called GLP-1 receptor agonists, which mimic a gut hormone to curb appetite and steady blood sugar. As tens of millions began taking them, researchers noticed benefits beyond weight, from the heart to the kidneys, and started asking a bigger question: could these drugs affect aging itself? To test that, scientists use 'epigenetic clocks' — lab tests that read chemical marks on DNA to estimate how fast a body is actually wearing down, regardless of age in years.

thread.previously: Yesterday we flagged this as a one-line notable — early research hinting the blockbuster weight-loss drugs might slow biological aging, a potentially huge story for health and pharma.
thread.changed: The actual trial is now out: a small UC San Diego randomized study found semaglutide slowed an epigenetic aging marker by about 9% over 32 weeks — the first human clinical hint, but in just 108 people with HIV-related fat changes, so far from proof.

stories.title: Sonam Wangchuk urged to end 17-day hunger strike over exam-paper leaks
stories.what_happened: Wangchuk has been on hunger strike at a protest site in central New Delhi since June 28, reaching his 17th day as his health deteriorates — reports say he has lost about 8.5 kg. He is fasting in solidarity with the founder of a Gen Z-led outfit, the 'Cockroach Janta Party', who is staging a sit-in demanding that Education Minister Dharmendra Pradhan resign over exam-paper leaks in May that affected millions of students. Opposition leaders, along with the protest's own organisers, have urged Wangchuk to end the fast as concern grows for his condition, but he has so far refused.
key_points: Activist fasting since June 28; now 17th day, health worsening
Demand: education minister resign over May exam-paper leaks
In solidarity with a Gen Z-led youth party's sit-in
Opposition leaders and organisers press him to call it off

depth.background: Sonam Wangchuk is a well-known engineer-turned-education-reformer from Ladakh whose life inspired the lead character in the hit Bollywood film 3 Idiots. He has repeatedly used peaceful protest and hunger strikes to draw attention to public causes. His current fast targets one of India's most sensitive nerves: exam integrity. Millions of students sit high-stakes national exams each year for university seats and government jobs, so a leaked question paper can wipe out years of preparation and hand an unfair edge to those who cheat — making paper leaks a recurring flashpoint for the country's exam-dependent youth.

stories.title: India's imports from China hit a record ~$80bn in the first half of 2026
stories.what_happened: New official trade data show India imported roughly $80 billion of goods from China in the first half of 2026 — the highest ever for that period — while exports to China rose about 37%, but from a far smaller base. Figures for April–June alone put imports near $38 billion, up around 28% on last year. The surge was led by electronics, machinery, industrial chemicals, solar equipment and components.
key_points: Imports from China reach a record ~$80bn in H1 2026
Exports to China rose ~37% but from a much smaller base
The trade gap keeps widening — India buys far more than it sells
Electronics, machinery, solar and chemicals led the import surge

depth.background: India and China are giant neighbours and rivals — their armies clashed on the Himalayan border in 2020, and relations have been frosty since. Yet India's factories, phone makers, solar plants and drug makers lean heavily on Chinese parts and materials. For years India has run a large trade deficit with China (it imports far more than it exports), and that gap has kept growing.

stories.title: India's wholesale inflation jumps to 9.87% in June, driven by mineral oils, food and metals
stories.source: The Hindu (WPI inflation rises to 9.87% in June; minerals, food items see price spike)
stories.what_happened: Ministry of Commerce data showed WPI inflation climbed to 9.87% in June from 9.68% in May, pushing wholesale prices to the edge of double digits. The rise was led by 'mineral oils' (petroleum products), food articles and basic metals. By group, fuel-and-power inflation ran hot at 27.41%, primary articles at 7.0%, and manufactured products at 7.48%; the WPI food index rose to 6.14% from 4.49% in May.
key_points: Wholesale inflation (WPI) rose to 9.87% in June, from 9.68% in May
Fuel and power prices up a steep 27.41% year-on-year
Mineral oils, food articles and basic metals led the rise
Wholesale prices often flow through to shop prices weeks later

depth.background: India tracks two different inflation gauges. Retail inflation (the CPI) measures what households pay at the shop. Wholesale inflation — the Wholesale Price Index (WPI) — measures the bulk, factory-gate prices businesses charge one another before goods reach the consumer. WPI is dominated by fuel, raw materials and manufactured goods, so it swings sharply with global oil and metal prices, and it often moves before retail prices do.

sources.name: The Hindu (WPI inflation rises to 9.87% in June; minerals, food items see price spike)
Business Standard (India's wholesale inflation rises to 9.87% in June on high food prices)

stories.title: Rupee breaches 96 to the dollar as crude climbs and the RBI's forex boost fades
stories.source: Business Recorder (Indian rupee may drift towards 96 on twin drag from oil and U.S. yields)
stories.what_happened: The rupee weakened past the 96-per-dollar mark, approaching record lows, after settling around 95.6 the previous session. The immediate triggers: a renewed round of US strikes on Iran reignited fears over oil supply and pushed Brent crude to its highest in more than a month (around $85 a barrel), while hawkish comments from a US Federal Reserve official lifted US Treasury yields — both drawing money toward the dollar and away from the rupee. The RBI had recently pulled in dollars via a special deposit drive to support the currency, but that boost is fading against the twin pressure. Traders expect the RBI to intervene to slow the slide rather than let the rupee hit a lifetime low immediately.
key_points: Rupee slid past 96 per US dollar, near record lows
A renewed US–Iran flare-up pushed oil to one-month highs (~$85)
Rising US bond yields pulled global money toward the dollar
RBI expected to intervene, but a weak rupee makes India's oil bill costlier

depth.background: The rupee's value against the US dollar matters enormously to India because it prices the country's imports — above all oil, which India buys in dollars and imports about 85% of. When the rupee falls (more rupees needed to buy one dollar), every barrel of oil costs more in rupee terms. Two forces usually push the rupee down: a rising oil price (India must buy more dollars to pay its import bill) and rising US interest rates/yields (which pull global money toward the dollar). Right now both are happening at once.

sources.name: Business Recorder (Indian rupee may drift towards 96 on twin drag from oil and U.S. yields)
The Logical Indian (Rupee slides to 95.95 against US dollar as oil prices surge amid Middle East tensions)

stories.title: New bids from Fairfax and Emirates NBD revive the stalled IDBI Bank privatisation
stories.what_happened: The stalled sale has been revived after Canada's Fairfax Financial Holdings and Dubai's Emirates NBD submitted revised financial bids for the controlling stake. Earlier bids in March fell short of the government's reserve (minimum) price, so authorities cut that price by around 20% to draw fresh interest. Officials say the revised bids are now being evaluated, with the sale potentially completed within a month.
key_points: Fresh bids from Fairfax (Canada) and Emirates NBD (Dubai) revive the sale
The government and LIC are selling a 60.7% controlling stake
Earlier March bids fell below the reserve price and stalled
The reserve price was cut ~20% to attract buyers

depth.background: IDBI Bank is a mid-sized Indian lender that the government has tried to privatise — sell to private owners — for years, as part of a broader plan to exit businesses the state need not run. The Centre owns about 45.5% and state-owned insurer LIC about 49.2%; together they aim to sell a 60.7% controlling stake with management control. The process has repeatedly stalled, most recently in March 2026 when bids came in below the government's minimum price.

stories.title: China's exports jump 27% in June as the AI boom drives chip demand - the other side of the AI trade
stories.source: AP News (China's June exports surge 27% from a year earlier as AI boom drives strong demand)
stories.what_happened: Customs data showed China's exports rose 27% from a year earlier in June - the fastest pace since 2021 and far above the ~18% economists expected, up from 19.4% in May. A customs vice-minister, Wang Jun, said AI is driving robust trade in the sector; Chinese companies exported 32 billion integrated circuits in the month. China's trade surplus (exports minus imports) widened to $125.6 billion from $105.4 billion. Exports to Southeast Asia jumped nearly 35%, and to the EU and Latin America more than 18% and 28% respectively. Imports rose 36% (partly on costlier goods amid the Iran war), and car exports topped one million vehicles in a single month for the first time.
key_points: China's June exports surged 27% year-on-year - fastest since 2021, well above the ~18% forecast
Chinese firms shipped 32 billion integrated circuits (chips) in June on AI demand
Trade surplus widened to $125.6bn; car exports topped 1 million in a month for the first time
Exports to Southeast Asia +35%, EU +18%, Latin America +28%

depth.background: China is the world's largest exporter, so its monthly trade data is a closely watched read on global demand. For two years an AI boom has driven surging demand for semiconductors (the chips that power computers and AI systems) and the hardware around them, and China both makes and re-exports large volumes of this gear - so a global scramble for AI hardware shows up directly in its export numbers. This print landed the same week IBM's shares crashed about 24% on a warning that AI-hardware spending is cannibalising its software sales - two sides of the same AI-spending wave.

sources.name: AP News (China's June exports surge 27% from a year earlier as AI boom drives strong demand)
AJOT (China's exports ride AI boom as domestic economy struggles)
China-Global South (China Exports Surge as AI Chip Demand Powers Trade)

stories.title: Yemen's Houthis strike Saudi Arabia's Abha airport with missiles and drones - the war widens onto Saudi soil
stories.source: AP News via The Washington Post (Yemen's Houthis strike Saudi Arabia's Abha airport with missiles and drones)
stories.what_happened: The Iran-aligned Houthis said they launched missiles and drones at Saudi Arabia's Abha International Airport on Monday, calling it retaliation for airstrikes they blamed on Saudi Arabia that hit Sanaa International Airport earlier that day. No casualties were reported; the Saudi-led coalition's spokesman, Maj. Gen. Turki al-Malki, said air defences dealt with ballistic missiles fired toward the kingdom's south. A Houthi military spokesman, Brig. Gen. Yahya Saree, warned airlines to avoid Saudi airspace 'until the blockade on Sanaa International Airport is lifted.' The UN Security Council held an emergency meeting, with a senior UN official warning the region 'cannot afford another cycle of escalation.'
key_points: Houthis fire missiles and drones at Saudi Arabia's Abha International Airport
Framed as retaliation for strikes they blame on Saudi Arabia at Sanaa airport
No casualties reported; Saudi air defences intercepted ballistic missiles
Houthis warn airlines off Saudi airspace; UN Security Council meets in emergency

depth.background: The Houthis are an armed movement that controls much of northern Yemen, including the capital Sanaa; they are aligned with Iran and have fought a Saudi-led coalition since it intervened in Yemen's civil war in 2015. Over those years they have periodically fired missiles and drones at Saudi Arabia. This strike is the sharpest such escalation seen since that coalition campaign years ago, and it plugs directly into the wider US-Iran war now raging across the Gulf.

sources.name: AP News via The Washington Post (Yemen's Houthis strike Saudi Arabia's Abha airport with missiles and drones)
Al Jazeera (Houthis: Saudi airport targeted in retaliation for strikes on Sanaa airport)
Business Standard (Houthis strike Saudi's Abha airport with missiles in sharp escalation)

thread.previously: Yesterday we reported a Houthi leader threatening a 'siege' on Saudi Arabia after strikes on Yemen's Sanaa airport, as the US-Iran war widened across the Gulf.
thread.changed: The Houthis turned the threat into action - firing missiles and drones at Saudi Arabia's Abha airport (intercepted, no casualties) and warning airlines off Saudi airspace, the sharpest escalation onto Saudi soil in years.

stories.title: Hungary's parliament votes to oust Orban's ally from the presidency
stories.what_happened: Parliament passed a constitutional amendment to remove President Sulyok, with 139 votes in favour and just 6 against, a move that would immediately end his term and clear the way to elect a replacement. Magyar framed it as 'an important step to eliminate Orban's economic and political mafia.' There is a catch: for the amendment to take legal effect it must be signed by Sulyok himself — and analysts and diplomats expect him to refuse, which could trigger a constitutional standoff.
key_points: MPs pass a constitutional change to remove President Tamas Sulyok
Lopsided vote: 139 in favour, only 6 against
New PM Peter Magyar is dismantling Orban's 16-year machine
Catch: Sulyok may refuse to sign, risking a constitutional crisis

depth.background: For 16 years Hungary was run by Viktor Orban, a nationalist who clashed repeatedly with the European Union over democracy, migration and the rule of law and cultivated warm ties with Russia. In April 2026 voters turned him out, handing a landslide to Peter Magyar and his pro-European, centre-right Tisza party, which now holds a two-thirds parliamentary majority — enough to rewrite the constitution. Tamas Sulyok, the president, is a largely ceremonial head of state but an Orban appointee, and one of the last levers of the old order still in place.

stories.title: War-risk hits the pump: Brent jumps ~10% to a one-month high as the Hormuz crisis flares, while AI chip stocks slide
stories.source: AP News (Oil prices jump following the latest fighting in the Middle East, while AI stocks sink)
stories.what_happened: After a weekend of US–Iran strikes and duelling claims over who controls the Strait of Hormuz, Brent crude — the international benchmark — surged about 9.6% to $83.30 a barrel, its highest in a month, with the gains accelerating immediately after President Trump said he was reinstating a blockade on Iranian oil tankers. As oil climbed, AI-linked shares slid: Nvidia — the most valuable US stock — fell 3.5% and was the single heaviest drag on the S&P 500, while memory-chip maker Micron dropped 4.4% (trimming a stellar 243% gain for the year). The S&P 500 fell 60 points to 7,515, the Dow dropped 138 to 52,499, and the Nasdaq sank 408 to 25,873.
key_points: Brent crude climbed ~9.6% to $83.30 — a one-month high — on July 13
Gains accelerated right after Trump reinstated the Hormuz blockade
AI winners led stocks lower: Nvidia −3.5%, Micron −4.4%
S&P 500, Dow and Nasdaq all fell as the two forces collided

depth.background: About a fifth of the world's seaborne oil passes through the Strait of Hormuz, so when fighting there disrupts tanker traffic, the price of crude — the raw material for petrol, diesel and jet fuel — jumps worldwide. At the same time, the market's biggest recent winners have been AI-linked chip stocks like Nvidia, which have soared for two years; because they have risen so far, they also fall fastest when fear returns. On July 13, both forces hit at once.

sources.name: AP News (Oil prices jump following the latest fighting in the Middle East, while AI stocks sink)
The Washington Post (Oil prices jump following the latest fighting in the Middle East, while AI stocks sink)
BNN Bloomberg (Oil prices jump and world shares are mixed as US and Iran carry out airstrikes)

stories.title: HCLTech beats estimates on financial-services strength and enters the data-centre business
stories.what_happened: HCLTech reported first-quarter (FY27) net profit up 20.3% to ₹4,624 crore on revenue of ₹34,579 crore, beating analyst estimates; dollar revenue was $3.65 billion, up 3% year-on-year. Growth was led by financial services, manufacturing and technology. It logged record first-quarter net new deal bookings of $2.4 billion and said it will invest ₹3,500 crore to enter the AI data-centre business, scalable to 50 megawatts. It kept its full-year revenue-growth guidance.
key_points: Q1 profit rose ~20% to ₹4,624 crore, beating estimates
Record first-quarter deal bookings of $2.4 billion
Entering the AI data-centre business with a ₹3,500-crore plan
Financial-services demand led the growth

depth.background: HCLTech is India's third-largest IT-services company, after TCS and Infosys, serving global banks, manufacturers and tech firms. India's IT giants are all scrambling to prove they can profit from the AI boom rather than be disrupted by it. 'Data centres' — warehouses of powerful computers that run AI and cloud services — are where much of that AI spending is going, and until now Indian IT firms mostly wrote software rather than owning this physical infrastructure.

thread.previously: In yesterday's markets roundup we flagged that IT was shining — HCL Tech Q1 profit up ~20% to about ₹4,626 crore with a ₹12 dividend — as earnings cushioned a jittery market.
thread.changed: Today we decode the full result: the estimate beat, record $2.4 billion first-quarter bookings, retained FY27 guidance, and a new ₹3,500-crore push into the AI data-centre business.

stories.title: A five-month-high trade gap: India's goods deficit widens to $30.4bn on a costlier oil bill and softer exports
stories.source: Reuters via Bloomberg (India trade deficit widens as Hormuz risks cloud outlook)
stories.what_happened: Government data showed India's merchandise (goods) trade deficit widened to about $30.4 billion in June - a five-month high, up from $28.2bn in May and far above the ~$19bn gap a year earlier, and bigger than the ~$26.5bn economists had expected. Imports jumped roughly 31% year-on-year to about $70.8 billion (a pricier oil bill, plus restocking of fertilisers, electronics and farm goods), while exports grew a slower 15.5% to about $40.4 billion. In short, India's purchases from the world grew much faster than its sales to it.
key_points: June goods trade deficit widened to a five-month high of ~$30.4bn
Imports rose ~31% to ~$70.8bn; exports rose 15.5% to ~$40.4bn
A bigger oil bill plus soft global demand drove the gap
A wider deficit pressures the rupee and adds to inflation

depth.background: A trade deficit is the gap when a country buys more goods from abroad (imports) than it sells (exports). India usually runs one, mainly because it imports huge amounts of crude oil and gold. To pay for those imports, Indians must sell rupees to buy dollars - so a wider goods gap tends to weaken the rupee. The Hormuz war has made oil dearer just as global demand for Indian exports has softened, widening that gap.

sources.name: Reuters via Bloomberg (India trade deficit widens as Hormuz risks cloud outlook)
Business Standard (India's trade deficit widens to five-month high of $30.43 billion in June)
ANI (India's trade deficit may stay elevated amid Middle East risks, oil prices)

thread.previously: On Monday we noted, inside India's market wrap, that the June trade deficit had widened to a five-month high of $30.43bn even as exports grew 15.5% - feeding the record-low rupee.
thread.changed: The focus now is on the deficit itself and its drivers - imports up ~31% on a dearer oil bill against softer 15.5% export growth - and how Hormuz-driven oil keeps the gap, and the pressure on the rupee, elevated.

stories.title: Volkswagen warns of up to 50,000 more job cuts to survive
stories.what_happened: In an internal memo, CEO Oliver Blume told staff that Volkswagen may need to cut about 50,000 more jobs on top of the roughly 50,000 already agreed — confirming for the first time it is weighing up to 100,000 reductions. Blume said the group carries a roughly 20% cost disadvantage versus comparable rivals and that, as of today, it cannot confirm a competitive future for four German plants — Emden, Hanover, Zwickau and Neckarsulm — in the 2030s. He said he prefers 'intelligent solutions' (like building Chinese-designed VWs in Europe, or defence work at idle sites) over outright factory closures.
key_points: CEO memo: another ~50,000 cuts on top of 50,000 already agreed
That's up to 100,000 jobs — near a fifth of Europe's biggest carmaker
Cause: Chinese EV rivals, tariffs, a slow and costly EV switch
Four German plants have no confirmed future in the 2030s

depth.background: Volkswagen is Europe's largest carmaker and a pillar of Germany's economy — its brands include VW, Audi, Porsche, Skoda and Seat, and the auto industry supports millions of German jobs directly and through suppliers. For decades VW's cash cow was China, but the world's biggest car market has flipped fast to electric vehicles (EVs) built by cheaper, quicker local champions such as BYD. VW has been slow and expensive making the same shift, and its profits have slumped under tariff costs and falling sales.

stories.title: India's retail inflation breaches the RBI's 4% target for the first time in 17 months — June CPI hits 4.38%
stories.source: Bloomberg (India inflation tops RBI target for first time in 17 months)
stories.what_happened: Government data showed the Consumer Price Index (CPI) — the main retail-inflation gauge — rose 4.38% in June from a year earlier, up from 3.93% in May and the highest reading since December 2024. It pushed past the RBI's 4% target for the first time in 17 months, though it stayed inside the 2–6% band. Food inflation was the main driver, climbing to 5.32% (with sharp spikes in items like tomatoes and ginger), alongside firmer transport costs. Forecasters such as ICRA still expect the RBI's rate-setting committee to hold rates steady at its August meeting rather than hike.
key_points: June retail inflation (CPI) rose to 4.38%, from 3.93% in May
First time above the RBI's 4% target in 17 months (highest since Dec 2024)
Still inside the RBI's 2–6% tolerance band
Food prices led the jump; most economists still expect a rate hold, not a hike

depth.background: The Reserve Bank of India (RBI), the country's central bank, is legally tasked with keeping retail inflation — the pace at which the prices households pay are rising — at around 4%, within a tolerance band of 2% to 6%. It uses interest rates to do this: raising them to cool inflation, cutting them to support growth. Inflation had stayed below the 4% midpoint for well over a year, giving the RBI room to keep rates low.

sources.name: Bloomberg (India inflation tops RBI target for first time in 17 months)
U.S. News / Reuters (India's retail inflation accelerates to 4.38%, raising rate-hike expectations)

stories.title: Negotiating from strength: India holds out for better terms even as the US trade deal is called 'ready'
stories.source: Business Today (India-US negotiations progressing well, framework deal is ready: Commerce Secretary)
stories.what_happened: India's Commerce Secretary Rajesh Agarwal said on July 13 that talks are 'progressing very well' and the framework deal is 'ready,' but pointedly declined to give a signing timeline. Behind that is a harder stance: Commerce Minister Piyush Goyal has said India will not sign unless the US gives it 'some competitive advantage' over rival exporters like Vietnam, Thailand and the Philippines. In other words, an emboldened India - confident in its fast growth and its widening web of other trade deals (the UK FTA now in force, plus New Zealand) - is willing to wait rather than accept the terms on the table.
key_points: India's Commerce Secretary says the framework deal is 'ready' - but gives no signing date
Delhi pulled back after US negotiators claimed 99% done
India wants an edge over rivals like Vietnam, Thailand and the Philippines
At stake: tariffs on billions of dollars of India-US goods trade

depth.background: India and the US have been negotiating a Bilateral Trade Agreement (BTA) for months. In February they announced an interim framework in which India would cut tariffs on US industrial and farm goods, while the US could apply a reciprocal tariff (reported around 18%, far above July 2025 levels) on Indian goods. In late June, US negotiators visited Delhi and said 99% of the deal was done - then India unexpectedly pulled back, deciding it could get better terms.

sources.name: Business Today (India-US negotiations progressing well, framework deal is ready: Commerce Secretary)
The Diplomat (Why is the India-US bilateral trade agreement still on hold?)

thread.previously: On Monday we reported an 'emboldened' India holding out for better US terms - with Goyal calling a report that India rejected a quick deal 'completely false' - while it signed a 'historic' FTA with New Zealand.
thread.changed: The hold-out has hardened: Commerce Secretary Rajesh Agarwal now says the framework is 'ready' but will not give a signing date, and Goyal is demanding a 'competitive advantage' over rivals like Vietnam and Thailand - a stance strengthened by the UK FTA coming into force.

stories.title: India's inflation jumps past the RBI's target for the first time in 18 months — pricier fuel and food push June prices up 4.38%
stories.what_happened: Government data released on July 13, 2026 showed retail inflation accelerated to 4.38% in June, up from 3.93% in May — breaching the RBI's 4% target for the first time since January 2025 and hitting an 18-month high. It also topped forecasts (economists polled had expected about 4.2%). Food inflation climbed to 5.32%, and the transport category jumped to 4.31% from 1.75% — June was the first full month to reflect the mid-May hikes in petrol and diesel prices. Rural inflation (4.74%) ran hotter than urban (3.92%).
key_points: June retail inflation rose to 4.38%, up from 3.93% in May
First time above the RBI's 4% target since January 2025
Costlier petrol, diesel and food did most of the damage
A patchy monsoon threatens to push food prices higher still

depth.background: 'Inflation' is the pace at which prices rise across the economy — measured in India by the Consumer Price Index (CPI), a fixed basket of everyday goods and services (food, fuel, rent, clothes) whose total cost is tracked each month. The Reserve Bank of India (RBI), the country's central bank, is legally tasked with keeping CPI inflation at 4%, within a 2–6% band. When inflation runs hot, the RBI tends to raise its main interest rate (the 'repo rate') to cool spending; when it's soft, it can cut rates to boost growth. For much of the past year inflation sat comfortably below 4%, giving the RBI room to support the economy.

stories.title: Over 2,700 may have died in England and Wales's May–June heatwaves
stories.what_happened: A rapid 'attribution' study — researchers including the London School of Hygiene & Tropical Medicine, using Met Office temperature records — estimated that more than 2,700 people died from heat-related causes across two heatwaves: about 550 during 21–29 May and about 2,200 during 18–28 June, in England and Wales. They calculated that human-caused climate change added roughly 3–4°C to the peak temperatures and was responsible for about 42% of the deaths. Those aged over 85 accounted for around 60% of the toll, with the South East, London and the West Midlands hardest hit.
key_points: Scientists estimate ~2,700 excess deaths across two heatwaves
About 42% of the deaths are blamed on human-caused warming
People over 85 made up roughly 60% of the toll
Heat kills quietly indoors — now a leading weather hazard

depth.background: Heat is the most underestimated weather killer — it doesn't level buildings like a storm, it stops vulnerable hearts and lungs quietly, often indoors. Britain is especially exposed because it was built for a cool, damp climate: most homes, care homes and hospitals have no air-conditioning, and bodies there aren't acclimatised to extreme heat. This summer the UK broke its May temperature record and then endured three straight record-breaking days in June.

thread.previously: As part of several days of heat-and-fire coverage across Europe and the US, we reported new data suggesting about 2,700 people may have died in England and Wales's exceptional May–June heatwaves.
thread.changed: The full attribution study now breaks the toll down — about 550 deaths in May and 2,200 in June, with roughly 42% of them blamed on human-caused warming and the over-85s making up around 60%.

stories.title: Bangkok's deadliest fire in 17 years: at least 32 killed at a packed live-music bar as victims are found trapped in windowless bathrooms
stories.what_happened: Shortly before midnight on Sunday, 12 July 2026, a fire broke out at the Rong Beer Na Ladprao bar in Bangkok. It took firefighters about half an hour to control the blaze, but the fire spread rapidly across a ceiling lined with flammable decorative material before reaching the main entrance. Many of the dead were found trapped in windowless bathrooms where they appear to have fled to escape the flames and smoke. The death toll, first reported at 27, has risen over the following days to at least 32, with dozens more injured and many still in intensive care.
key_points: Fire tore through the Rong Beer Na Ladprao bar late Sunday
Death toll has climbed to at least 32, dozens still hospitalised
Many victims died trapped in windowless toilets seeking escape
A flammable ceiling and blocked exits turned it into a death trap

depth.background: Thailand, and Bangkok in particular, has a long record of deadly entertainment-venue fires — the worst being the 2009 Santika nightclub blaze that killed 66 people, roughly 17 years ago. After each disaster the government promises tighter enforcement of fire codes, but crowded bars with flammable decor, few marked exits and no sprinklers keep reappearing. The Rong Beer Na Ladprao was a popular live-music bar in the north of the capital that advertised space for as many as 600 customers.

stories.title: TCS to build up to 8,900 'AI-deployment' engineers and eyes AI acquisitions
stories.what_happened: TCS told Reuters it will convert 1% to 1.5% of its associates — roughly 5,900 to 8,900 people — into 'forward-deployed' AI engineers who sit inside client companies and build and tailor AI tools to each firm's systems. CEO K Krithivasan said TCS is also evaluating acquisitions in AI, data security and cybersecurity, after years of avoiding big M&A. Its AI work already runs at about a $1.5 billion annual rate, though quarterly growth has slowed from 28% to 13%.
key_points: TCS will build a team of up to 8,900 'forward-deployed' AI engineers
That is 1–1.5% of its associates retrained to embed AI inside clients
It is hunting acquisitions in AI, data security and cybersecurity
A bet that AI creates new work rather than killing outsourcing

depth.background: Tata Consultancy Services is India's largest IT firm and one of the world's biggest tech-outsourcing companies, with over 600,000 employees who build and run software for global banks, retailers and manufacturers. Its model has long been selling large teams of engineers by the hour. AI now threatens that model — if software can be written and maintained with far fewer people, the old 'headcount-for-billing' approach is at risk.

stories.title: First-ever treatment trial for this Ebola strain begins at record speed
stories.what_happened: The WHO-backed PARTNERS trial — short for a 'platform adaptive randomised trial', a flexible study that can test several drugs at once and add or drop them as data arrives — enrolled its first patients in the DRC, testing two candidates: MBP134, a lab-made antibody designed to neutralise the virus, and remdesivir, an antiviral. It was set up in about six weeks, a record for this kind of research, after the WHO declared the outbreak a 'public health emergency of international concern' on 17 May. Gilead donated the remdesivir and the US government the antibody — enough for up to 1,200 patients. The outbreak is the largest Bundibugyo episode on record, with roughly 454 deaths among about 1,481 cases.
key_points: WHO-backed PARTNERS trial enrolls first patients against a live outbreak
Two drugs tested: antibody MBP134 and antiviral remdesivir
Stood up in just ~6 weeks after WHO declared a global emergency
Outbreak has killed ~454; no drug has ever been proven for this strain

depth.background: Ebola is a severe, often-fatal virus spread through the bodily fluids of infected people; it causes internal bleeding and organ failure and was first identified near the DRC's Ebola River in 1976. There are several species of the virus. Two approved treatments exist — but only for the Zaire species. The current outbreak is the Bundibugyo species, which has sickened people since 2007 and against which no drug has ever been formally tested. Running such a trial is hard because outbreaks are remote, fast-moving and dangerous.

thread.previously: On July 1 we reported this DRC Ebola outbreak had passed 300 deaths and was on track to be one of the deadliest in recent history, and flagged vaccination and contact-tracing as the things to watch.
thread.changed: The toll has since climbed to around 454 deaths, the strain is confirmed as Bundibugyo, and the response has shifted from containment toward treatment: a first-ever, record-fast drug trial for this strain has begun enrolling patients.

stories.title: China evacuates nearly two million as Typhoon Bavi hits Zhejiang
stories.what_happened: Typhoon Bavi made landfall near the city of Yuhuan in Zhejiang province late Saturday, weakening from a full typhoon to a tropical storm but remaining enormous. Authorities evacuated more than 2.2 million people in Zhejiang alone, plus over 180,000 in neighbouring Fujian and roughly 290,000 in Shanghai, and suspended schools, work and much transport. The storm felled more than 1,300 trees in Yueqing, and cancelled hundreds of flights and over 1,600 trains across the region.
key_points: ~2.2 million evacuated in Zhejiang; hundreds of thousands more nearby
Bavi weakened to a tropical storm at landfall but stayed vast
Rain bands ~1,000km wide — roughly the length of France
Hundreds of flights and 1,600+ trains cancelled

depth.background: The western Pacific is in the peak of typhoon season — typhoons are the same kind of storm the Atlantic calls a hurricane. Warmer ocean water is the fuel these storms feed on, so hotter seas tend to make them bigger, wetter and more intense. Bavi struck China's densely populated, factory-heavy east coast — a region that sits near Taiwan, home to the world's most advanced computer-chip factories — and was the country's second major typhoon in a single week.

thread.previously: Over several days we tracked Bavi crossing Taiwan and nearing landfall in Zhejiang, with evacuations scaled up toward two million and 15 killed earlier in Philippine landslides.
thread.changed: Bavi has now come ashore near Yuhuan and Wenzhou, weakening from a typhoon to a tropical storm; final tallies show 2.2 million evacuated in Zhejiang plus hundreds of thousands more in Fujian and Shanghai, with mass flight and train cancellations.

stories.title: Apple sues OpenAI, alleging a stolen-secrets shortcut into AI hardware
stories.what_happened: Apple filed a lawsuit in a US federal court in Northern California accusing OpenAI of stealing 'trade secrets' — confidential know-how that gives a company its edge. Apple says the scheme reached 'every level' and was directed by OpenAI's Chief Hardware Officer Tang Tan, who spent 24 years at Apple, most recently as VP of product design for the iPhone and Watch. Among the claims: Tan urged Apple staff he was recruiting to bring 'actual parts' from Apple to their interviews for 'show and tell'; he emailed himself details of Apple's suppliers before leaving; departing staff were allegedly coached to dodge Apple's exit-security checks; and one former employee is accused of stealing an Apple laptop. Apple wants damages and a court order forcing OpenAI to stop using the secrets. OpenAI has not conceded the claims, which remain untested in court.
key_points: Apple accuses OpenAI of poaching staff to steal iPhone-making secrets
Central figure: OpenAI hardware chief Tang Tan, a 24-year Apple veteran
Claims include recruits bringing real Apple parts to job interviews
Two AI-era titans now in open court, not just competition

depth.background: Apple, the world's most valuable company, built its fortune on tightly-guarded hardware — the iPhone, Watch and AirPods. OpenAI, the maker of ChatGPT, has been racing beyond software into physical devices: in 2025 it bought io, the startup founded by former Apple design legend Jony Ive, and hired dozens of ex-Apple engineers to build an AI-first gadget. That hiring spree has now curdled into a courtroom fight.

stories.title: India's most famous central banker lands a US seat: ex-RBI chief Raghuram Rajan is co-leading a task force to overhaul the Federal Reserve
stories.what_happened: On July 9 the new Fed Chair, Kevin Warsh, announced five expert 'task forces' to overhaul how the Fed works — covering its communications, its balance sheet, its use of economic data, jobs and productivity, and its inflation framework. Rajan was named a co-chair of the balance-sheet task force alongside Harvard economist Karen Dynan and former Fed governor Jeremy Stein. Indian outlets reported that Rajan is among a small group of Indian-origin economists Warsh has drawn on for the review.
key_points: Ex-RBI Governor Raghuram Rajan tapped for a US Fed reform panel
He co-chairs the group rethinking the Fed's balance sheet
New Fed Chair Kevin Warsh has launched five task forces
Findings due later in 2026 could reshape global interest rates

depth.background: Raghuram Rajan ran India's central bank, the Reserve Bank of India (RBI), from 2013 to 2016 — taming double-digit inflation, steadying a sliding rupee, and helping put India on a modern rate-setting footing. Earlier, as chief economist of the IMF, he famously warned in 2005 that the financial system was becoming dangerously risky, three years before the 2008 crash. He is now a finance professor at the University of Chicago. The US Federal Reserve — America's central bank, whose interest-rate moves ripple through every market on earth — is now rethinking how it operates after inflation ran above its target for more than five years.
